===== Song2018_NatImmunol+microglia =====
Review Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41590-018-0212-1
Department of Pathology and Immunology, Washington University School of Medicine, Saint Louis, MO, USA. *e-mail: mcolonna@wustl.edu
T
he immune system has long been recognized as a driver or 
modifier of neuroinflammatory conditions such as infection, 
stroke and certain autoimmune diseases. Emerging evidence 
now links the function of the immune system to a variety of chronic 
neurodegenerative diseases1–4. The main type of immune cell in the 
central nervous system (CNS) in these settings is the microglia, an 
embryonically derived5, self-renewing6–8 tissue macrophage. The 
maturation of microglia requires the lineage-specific transcrip-
tion factors Irf89 and Sall110 and input from the CNS microenvi-
ronment and microbiota11,12. The confluence of these factors drives 
a unique transcriptional signature that includes genes encoding 
surface receptors such as P2ry12, Tmem119 and Trem2, in addi-
tion to those encoding the tissue macrophage receptors CX3CR1, 
Mer and Fcgr113. To maintain their unique transcriptional identity, 
microglia require continuous signals from the microenvironment, 
including signals from the cytokine TGF-β​14; this leads to subtle dif-
ferences between microglia in different brain regions15. However, 
the brain microenvironment is not sufficient for microglial identity. 
Peripheral monocytes introduced into the brain parenchyma can 
differentiate into microglia-like cells, but they fail to fully upregu-
late genes expressed by homeostatic microglia16,17. Greater under-
standing of fundamental microglia biology during development 
and homeostasis over the past few years has helped to contextualize 
the responses of microglia to pathological insults. In this Review, 
we discuss the nature of the microglial response to neurodegenera-
tive diseases and the actions of activated microglia in promoting or 
abating disease progression.
Neurodegenerative diseases induce a common microglial 
signature
Microglia proliferate and change their morphology in neurode-
generative diseases, a process referred to as ‘reactive microgliosis’. 
In post-mortem specimens from people with Alzheimer’s disease 
(AD), these ‘reactive microglia’ are found in abundance surround-
ing pathological amyloid-β​ (Aβ​) plaques18, and ‘-omics’ studies 
of the human brain have revealed that changes in microglia con-
stitute the strongest transcriptional and epigenetic signals of AD 
and normal aging19–21. Reactive microglia are also found in brains 
from people with Parkinson’s disease (PD)22 or Huntington’s disease 
(HD)23. The most common approach to modeling AD in mice is 
to use transgenic systems that reproduce either of the two charac-
teristic AD pathologies: Aβ​ plaques or aggregates of tau protein. 
Aβ​ plaques are generated by the overexpression of human genes 
encoding the Aβ​ precursor protein APP and/or the Aβ​-process-
ing protein PS1, carrying single or multiple mutations that cause 
familial AD (‘Aβ​-transgenic’ models), while aggregates of tau pro-
tein are generated by overexpression of the human gene encod-
ing tau protein (MAPT), carrying mutations that cause familial 
tauopathy (‘tauopathy’ models). Both Aβ​-transgenic models and 
tauopathy models feature changes in reactive microglia similar to 
those seen in human neurodegeneration24,25, as do mouse models 
of PD26 and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)27. For many years, 
these degeneration- or disease-associated microglia (DAM) were 
vaguely described as having less-ramified, more-amoeboid mor-
phology and expressing markers of general macrophage activation, 
such as MHCII and CD11c. The development of techniques for the 
isolation of microglia and for genome-wide transcriptomic analy-
sis has allowed finer characterization of DAM. Remarkably, DAM 
have a conserved transcriptional signature across mouse models 
of AD, such as the APPswe/PSEN1dE9 and 5XFAD Aβ​-transgenic 
models28,29 and the P301S tauopathy model30. DAM are also found 
in other standard mouse models of neurodegeneration, including 
the SOD1G93A model of ALS31 generated by overexpression of the 
human gene encoding the enzyme SOD1 (superoxide dismutase 
1), carrying a mutation that results in the G93A substitution that 
causes familial ALS); and the CK-p25 model of AD, generated by 
inducible overexpression of the p25 form of the kinase regulator 
CDK5R1 in neurons to hyperactivate the kinase CDK5 and cause 
neuron death and deposition of Aβ​)32. The transcriptional signature 
of DAM includes genes characteristically expressed by both classical 
M1 macrophages and classical M2 macrophages, as well as mod-
ules for the interferon response, stress response, lysosomal function, 
and lipid metabolism. The genes that are upregulated include Axl, 
Clec7a, Cst7, Spp1, Gpnmb, Lgals3, Apoe and Trem2, among many 
others. The expression of genes encoding surface proteins such as 
CD11c, MHCII, CD44, CD14, CD86, CD39, CD90 and CD274 
(PD-L1) is also upregulated in DAM, and this has been validated 
at the protein level by mass cytometry33,34. Conversely, the signature 
The identity and function of microglia in 
neurodegeneration
Wilbur M. Song and Marco Colonna   *
The predominant type of immune cell in the brain is the microglia, a type of tissue-resident macrophage. In a variety of neurode-
generative settings, microglia alter their transcriptional profile, morphology and function in similar ways; thus, these activated 
cells have been called ‘degeneration- or disease-associated microglia’ (DAM). These activated microglia can perform different 
functions and exert both positive effects and negative effects in different mouse disease models. In humans, mutations in genes 
expressed in microglia are linked to various neurodegenerative diseases. Here we provide an overview of the common microg-
lial response to neurodegeneration and key contributing pathways; delineate the multifaceted functions of activated microglia 
spanning various diseases; and discuss insights from the study of human disease-associated genes. We argue that strong 
evidence from both mouse models and human genetics causally links the function of activated microglia to neurodegeneration.
Nature Immunology | VOL 19 | OCTOBER 2018 | 1048–1058 | www.nature.com/natureimmunology
1048

Review Article
NATure IMMunology
genes of homeostatic microglia are downregulated. Although it is 
shared by neurodegeneration models, the DAM signature is not 
simply a generalized response to any stimulus. In experimental auto-
immune encephalitis, which involves profuse inflammatory infil-
trates, microglial populations have surface-marker, cytokine and 
signaling profiles distinct from those of neurodegeneration mod-
els33,34, while systemic injection of lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which 
causes acute neuroinflammation, leads to a transcriptional signa-
ture that is different, overall35. Several meta-analyses of microglial 
gene-expression datasets have also identified distinctions between 
the responses of neuroinflammatory microglia and those of neuro-
degenerative microglia30,35.
One important question is whether the DAM compartment con-
sists of a homogeneous population or multiple distinct populations 
of M1, M2 and other subsets. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis of 
microglia in the 5XFAD36 and CK-p2532 mouse models of AD has 
shown that microglia coordinately acquire the entire DAM tran-
scriptional signature and, according to unbiased clustering analysis, 
form a singular, although heterogeneous, population. Several large-
scale mass cytometry studies, while powerful enough to distinguish 
meningeal macrophages, perivascular macrophages and peripheral 
monocytes, in addition to microglia, have also failed to identify mul-
tiple subsets of DAM33,34. However, a separate study did distinguish 
relatively minor subsets of CD44+ DAM and CXCR4+ DAM by flow 
cytometry37, indicative of the segregation of at least some DAM 
modules. Moreover, analysis of the heterogeneity of the DAM clus-
ter in the CK-p25 model has indicated that the expression of certain 
genes induced by type I interferons is inversely correlated with the 
expression of genes induced by type II interferon. Thus, DAM prob-
ably represent one population with some heterogeneity. It remains 
to be seen whether the most dissimilar DAM within this population 
have differences in function that are consequential in vivo.
While the DAM signature has been best defined through the use 
of models of AD and ALS, it also appears in non-neurodegener-
ative states. Some examples of this include treatment with cupri-
zone, which induces demyelination38; aged mice35 and the model 
of DNA damage–accelerated aging generated by deficiency in the 
DNA-excision–repair protein Ercc139; and acute degeneration of 
facial nuclei after facial-nerve axotomy7. The DAM signature is 
found 16 hours after intracranial injection of apoptotic neurons40. 
Perhaps most surprisingly, a DAM-like population is found in 
myelin-rich regions of the brain during the first postnatal week in 
wild-type mice41. Thus, the DAM state might represent a relatively 
rapid response to a set of stimuli that happen to accrue in aging 
and chronic disease and transiently occur during development. 
Comparison of the pathological features of these diverse models has 
provided some clues about what these stimuli could be. The P301S, 
SOD1G93A and facial-nerve-axotomy models result in widespread 
neuronal death7,25,42. Treatment with cuprizone induces the death of 
oligodendrocytes and the accumulation of myelin debris38. During 
both aging and development, apoptotic cells and disordered myelin 
are detected in the brain. DAM might be responding specifically 
to apoptotic cells; more generally to certain lipidic structures pres-
ent not only on apoptotic cells but also on myelin or protein aggre-
gate–associated apolipoproteins; or, even more generally, to the 
phagocytosis of any endogenous materials via lysosomal signaling. 
In summary, the DAM signature seems to be induced by various 
brain-intrinsic changes, in the absence of massive peripheral infil-
trates, and can occur on acute to chronic time scales (Fig. 1).
TREM2 and downstream targets are required for the 
induction of DAM
The molecule that has been most directly linked to activation of 
DAM is TREM2, a single-immunoglobulin-domain-containing 
macrophage-specific receptor. Naive TREM2-deficient mice have 
only very mild differences in the transcriptome, morphology and 
frequency of microglia relative to those of naive wild-type mice; 
however, in response to Aβ​, TREM2-deficient 5XFAD microglia 
fail to upregulate most genes characteristically expressed by DAM38. 
At the single-cell level, very few TREM2-deficient microglia acti-
vate to the same extent that wild-type DAM do, which suggests 
that TREM2-deficient microglia are inherently limited in their 
capacity for activation36. A similar dependence on TREM2 for the 
transition of microglia to the DAM phenotype has been found in 
models of cuprizone treatment38, P301S43, SODG93A 40 and injection 
of apoptotic neurons40. In contrast, TREM2 deficiency does not 
block the brain’s inflammatory response to endotoxin in vivo44 and 
enhances the responses of the transcription factor NF-κ​B in vitro45. 
Thus, TREM2 seems to be required specifically for induction of the 
DAM program.
Two competing paradigms for the function of TREM2 in DAM 
have emerged. The first model proposes that TREM2 broadly 
sustains energy production in microglia to facilitate activation 
triggered by other pathways. Unlike wild-type microglia, TREM2-
deficient microglia in the 5XFAD model enter a starvation-like 
state of low activity of the metabolic checkpoint kinase mTOR and 
increased autophagy while failing to upregulate DAM-associated 
genes. Enhancing energy utilization non-specifically with dietary 
cyclocreatine restores the function of mTOR, decreases autophagy 
and restores expression of the DAM marker Spp146. Energy supple-
mentation is effective in TREM2-deficient 5XFAD brains but not 
in TREM2-sufficient or non-5XFAD brains46, which hints that the 
TREM2–mTOR axis for energy production is necessary but not 
sufficient for activation. Moreover, effector pathways of TREM2, 
such as PI3K–Akt–mTOR, are not known to drive interferon-stim-
ulated genes on their own, despite the dependence of such genes on 
TREM2 in DAM. The second model proposes that TREM2 signals 
in response to neurodegeneration-relevant molecules to directly 
initiate the DAM program. In support of this model is the observa-
tion that the induction of DAM is dependent on the expression of 
TREM2 after acute insults such as axonal injury or the injection of 
apoptotic neurons, experimental systems in which microglia might 
not have enough time to enter a starvation-like state40. Furthermore, 
intrathecal injection of a TREM2-agonistic antibody induces the 
production of inflammatory cytokines and neuropathic pain in a 
manner dependent on the adaptor DAP1247. More work is needed 
to determine if the activation of TREM2 is sufficient to activate 
microglia and, if so, whether these activated microglia are DAM.
Once the DAM signature has been engaged, certain upregulated 
molecules might form an autocrine or paracrine loop to sustain it 
(Fig. 1b). Apoe (which encodes apolipoprotein E (ApoE)) is among 
the DAM-associated genes with the highest expression. Total ApoE 
deficiency diminishes the DAM signature in Aβ​ models40,48, and total 
or conditional deletion of ApoE in microglia diminishes the DAM 
signature after acute injection of apoptotic neurons40, although not 
as extensively as does TREM2 deficiency. These results are con-
sistent with reports that ApoE and clusterin (ApoJ) are ligands of 
TREM249–51 and might indicate that ApoE is one of the main ligands 
of TREM2 in the brain during neurodegeneration. TREM2 is also 
upregulated in DAM, which furthers an ApoE–TREM2 positive-
feedback loop. Another potential source of positive feedback is 
upregulation of the macrophage growth factor CSF-1 in DAM. 
This might locally boost signaling via the CSF-1 receptor (CSF1R), 
which can overlap and interact with TREM2 signaling52. The DAM-
specific secreted molecule Spp1 is a ligand for CD4453, which 
is upregulated by DAM; this represents another potential loop. 
Another gene expressed by DAM encodes the microRNA miR-155, 
which further shapes the DAM signature54; this is upregulated in 
SOD1G93A microglia dependent on both TREM2 and ApoE and pro-
motes loss of expression of genes in homeostatic microglia. Thus, a 
defining feature of DAM is their dependence on TREM2 signaling 
as well as certain TREM2-dependent genes.
Nature Immunology | VOL 19 | OCTOBER 2018 | 1048–1058 | www.nature.com/natureimmunology
1049

Review Article
NATure IMMunology
Type I and type II interferons and DAM
Type I interferon– and type II interferon–response genes are one 
of the most prominent modules of the DAM signature (Fig. 1a). 
At the single-cell level, expression of these genes shows an inverse 
correlation, which suggests that individual microglia might be 
exposed ‘preferentially’ to type I or type II interferons32. Antibody 
blockade of or microglia-specific deletion of the type I interferon 
receptor IFNAR decreases the number of microglia and abolishes 
the upregulation of type I interferon–response genes observed in 
several models. These include aging55,56; treatment with the neu-
rotoxin pro-drug MPTP, which causes loss of dopaminergic neu-
rons and is used to model PD26; and the APPswe/PSEN1dE9 mouse 
model of AD57. Increased expression of mRNAs encoding type I 
interferons themselves has also been observed after treatment with 
MPTP and in patients with PD26, and increased expression of type 
I interferons at the protein level has been observed in APPswe/
PSEN1dE9 mice and in patients with AD, which provides valida-
tion of the signature detected in DAM58. These studies provide solid 
evidence for an elevation in type I interferons in the brain during 
neurodegeneration, which affects the microglia response. However, 
overexpression of type I interferons is not sufficient to drive expres-
sion of the non–interferon-related modules in the DAM signa-
ture56, which indicates that microglia respond to additional stimuli 
during neurodegeneration.
The type II interferon IFN-γ​ has been extensively studied as 
a classical M1-polarizing agent in conjunction with signaling 
through the Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and NF-κ​B. IFN-γ​ deficiency 
decreases microgliosis in the Tg2576 Aβ​-transgenic mouse model 
and after treatment with MPTP, which shows that endogenous IFN-
γ​ has a role in the DAM response59,60. Meanwhile, Aβ​ has been pos-
tulated to activate TLR signaling through the pattern-recognition 
receptors CD36–TLR4–TLR661,62 or, alternatively, through TLR263. 
M1-polarized microglia stimulated by a combination of IFN-γ​ 
and TLR ligands can cause devastating neuronal damage via radi-
cal oxygen and nitrogen species produced by microglia64. However, 
Aβ​-transgenic models do not typically show such extensive neuron 
loss, suggestive of an absence of M1 microglia in these models. The 
effects of deleting TLR263 or TLR465 or their common signaling 
adaptor MyD8866,67 in Aβ​ mouse models have been variable, while 
the NF-κ​B pathway does not seem to be robustly activated in the 
R6/2 mouse model of HD, generated by overexpression of a frag-
ment of huntingtin that carries HD-causing glutamine repeats33. 
Thus, while IFN-γ​ signaling is probably induced across the vari-
ous DAM-inducing models, TLR–NF-κ​B signaling and neurotoxic 
M1 microglia might be orthogonal to the induction of DAM and 
might lead to inflammation-dependent neuronal loss in some 
models, such as treatment with MPTP, but not in others, such as 
Aβ​-transgenic models.
In summary, detailed analysis of microglia transcriptomes over 
the past few years has shifted the focus of microglia-activation stud-
ies away from the M1 and M2 paradigm and toward a conserved 
signature associated with neurodegeneration (DAM) that is adopted 
en bloc by activated microglia in diverse conditions. Several key reg-
ulators of the transition from homeostatic microglia to DAM have 
been identified, including TREM2–DAP12 and ApoE and prob-
ably type I and type II interferons. Additionally, loss of the critical 
homeostatic signal from TGF-β​ by itself seems to induce expression 
of some of the genes associated with DAM, including those encod-
ing MHCII and the invariant chain CD7468. The characterization 
of individual DAM-associated genes in different neuropathological 
settings will help confer meaning onto the DAM signature, and the 
characterization of isolated microglia, either in bulk or at single-
cell resolution, will help define the breadth of pathologies associated 
with DAM. Validating the existence of DAM in humans is especially 
important for this.
Modulation of pathological protein aggregates by 
microglia
Given the conserved nature of the DAM signature, microglia proba-
bly perform similar functions across diverse disease states. However, 
the net effect of DAM on disease progression might differ among 
mouse models and, more importantly, between mice and humans. 
Thus, it is useful to consider the role of microglia in neurodegenera-
tive disease from two complementary angles: mechanistic studies 
of DAM function through the use of controlled mouse models, and 
studies bridging those mechanisms to human genetic risk.
Unlike most neurodegeneration-associated protein aggregates, 
Aβ​ plaques form in the extracellular space and thus represent the 
most obvious opportunity for direct targeting by microglia (Fig. 2a). 
Immature, non-fibrillar Aβ​ plaques can be found in abundance in 
people with no cognitive symptoms; these plaques neither attract 
microglia nor cause neurite dystrophy69. On the contrary, fibrillar 
Aβ​ plaques containing a congophilic core are generally surrounded 
by microglia and by scattered dystrophic neurites. These surround-
ing microglia can wall off and compact fibrillar plaques to diminish 
the exposure of nearby neurons to Aβ​ and thereby protect them. 
In TREM2-, DAP12- or ApoE-deficient mice, which have fewer 
plaque-associated microglia than do their wild-type counterparts 
in Aβ​-transgenic models, fibrillar Aβ​ plaques have a more complex 
morphology, a greater surface area and more surrounding neurite

Fig. 1 | DAM are a unique population present in various neuropathologies. a, DAM are induced by a variety of stimuli (along perimeter at left), including 
protein aggregates, apoptotic cells and myelin debris, and require signaling by type I interferons (IFN-α​ and IFN-β​ (IFN-α​/β​)) and type II interferon (IFN-γ​). 
The DAM signature is normally suppressed by TGF-β​ and NRROS. DAM downregulate microglia signature genes and upregulate a common activation 
signature dependent on TREM2. b, Several factors secreted by DAM, including CSF-1, ApoE and Spp1, can form an autocrine loop by activating the  
DAM-expressed receptors CSF1R, TREM2 and CD44, respectively. Dashed arrows indicate links that have not been formally proven.

NATure IMMunology
dystrophy48,70,71. Microglia might also aid in clearing Aβ​, a process 
that has been extensively studied and reviewed72–74. Microglia can 
secrete proteinases, such as neprilysin, insulin-degrading enzyme 
and MMP9, that directly degrade soluble Aβ​75–77. More generally, 
microglia are presumed to phagocytose and degrade Aβ​. The scav-
enger receptor CD36 and TREM2 have been proposed as direct 
phagocytic receptors for Aβ​61,78 or Aβ​–lipoprotein complexes49 that 
direct the lysosomal degradation of Aβ​.
Microglia clearly have a latent ability to clear Aβ​, an ability that 
has become evident through various experimental manipulations. 
In Aβ​-transgenic models, viral overexpression of the pro-inflam-
matory cytokines IFN-γ​, TNF or IL-6 drives microgliosis, with 
a concomitant decrease in Aβ​79–81, while deficiency in the anti-
inflammatory cytokine IL-10 decreases Aβ​, and its overexpression 
increases Aβ​82,83. Conversely, diminishing inflammation in other 
ways, such as inhibiting the activation of inflammasomes, can 
promote the phagocytosis of Aβ​84. Other, more ‘exotic’ treatments, 
including scanning ultrasound85 and 40-Hz entrainment of neuro-
nal circuits via optogenetics or flashing light86, seem to mitigate the 
accumulation of Aβ​ by promoting phagocytosis. A more specific 
approach for targeting plaques is to administer antibodies to plaque 
components. Monthly systemic injection of aducanumab, an anti-
body to Aβ​, has been shown to shrink Aβ​ plaques in patients with 
AD, and accompanying experiments with Aβ​-transgenic mice sug-
gest that the administration of aducanumab boosts the number of 
plaque-associated microglia87. An antibody to ApoE4 also decreases 
the Aβ​ load and increases the number of microglia in a fashion 
dependent on the immunoglobulin receptor Fcγ​R88.
Despite the potential for microglia to clear Aβ​ when appropri-
ately stimulated, several studies have called into question whether 
endogenous microglial function really decreases the level of Aβ​. 
Replacement of microglia with monocyte-derived macrophages 
does not affect the accumulation of Aβ​ in various Aβ​-transgenic 
models89,90. Loss of TREM2, which abolishes the DAM signature 
and attenuates the microglial response to plaques, has inconsistent 
effects on the Aβ​ load29,91. In 5XFAD mice, depletion of microglia 
via PLX3397, a small-molecule inhibitor of CSF1R, does not alter 
the Aβ​ load92. In contrast, long-term, high-dose administration of 
PLX3397 in this model significantly lowers both soluble Aβ​ and 
insoluble Aβ​93. As mentioned above, the accumulation of Aβ​ is 
exacerbated by the activation of microglia via inflammasomes84,94. 
Specks of the NLRP3 inflammasome component ASC formed dur-
ing activation of the inflammasome are found in the core of Aβ​ 
plaques in both mouse specimens and human specimens and can 
accelerate the aggregation of Aβ​ in vitro and in vivo95. Microglia can 
also amplify the pathology of tauopathy. Depletion of microglia via

Fig. 2 | DAM respond to both extracellular insults and intraneuronal insults. a, In response to extracellular insults, DAM can clear apoptotic cells, myelin 
debris, amyloid plaques and, potentially, certain intraneuronal protein aggregates by phagocytosis. This function can be enhanced by plaque-targeting 
molecules such as antibodies to ApoE (ApoE Ab) or to Aβ​ (Aβ​ Ab) and can be inhibited by IL-10. DAM can form a barrier around amyloid plaques 
to reduce plaque toxicity, degrade Aβ​ via secreted proteases and affect plaque composition by secreting molecules such as ApoE or soluble TREM2 
(sTREM2) that are known to be contained within plaques. b, In response to neuronal damage, DAM can phagocytose live neurons via TAM receptors. They 
can secrete complement components and eliminate complement-bound synapses. Furthermore, they can secrete inflammatory cytokines that directly 
injure neurons or induce astrocytes to secrete distinct neurotoxic factors.
Nature Immunology | VOL 19 | OCTOBER 2018 | 1048–1058 | www.nature.com/natureimmunology
1051

Review Article
NATure IMMunology
PLX3397 reduces the spread of injected or exogenously expressed 
tau to other brain regions. Exosomes from tau-conditioned microg-
lia transmit tau better than unprocessed tau, which suggests that 
microglia facilitate the propagation of tau96.
In a few other settings, microglia do seem to aid in cleaning up 
toxic debris. Both in the cuprizone-treatment model and during 
postnatal development, microglia promote the formation of func-
tional myelin. In the cuprizone-treatment model, microglia clear 
up myelin debris in a CX3CR1- and TREM2-dependent manner to 
allow remyelination38,97,98. During development, microglial expres-
sion of the DAM protein IGF-1 boosts the production of myelin 
proteins41. In a mouse model with inducible expression of an aggre-
gation-prone mutant of the neurodegeneration-associated protein 
TDP-43, microglia seem to be oblivious to toxic intraneuronal TDP-
43 inclusions while they accumulate. However, once expression of 
the mutant TDP-43 is halted, they adopt a DAM signature and pro-
mote clearance of those inclusions, which leads to improved func-
tional outcomes99. Thus, microglia can interact in various ways with 
extracellular protein aggregates and have been shown in at least one 
instance to help clear intracellular aggregates.
Elimination of synapses and neurons by microglia
During development, microglia are essential both for the proper 
pruning of neuronal synapses and for the efferocytosis of nor-
mally occurring apoptotic neurons. However, these functions can 
be inappropriately engaged during neurodegeneration, which leads 
to excessive loss of neurons and synapses that precipitates or wors-
ens cognitive decline (Fig.  2b). Synaptic pruning by microglia is 
dependent on classical complement components100,101 and is modu-
lated by neuronal activity, by the interaction between CX3CR1 and 
its ligand CX3CL1, and by TREM2102–104. In the J20 Aβ​-transgenic 
mouse model and after injection of oligomeric Aβ​ into mice, syn-
aptic pruning by microglia is increased in a complement-dependent 
fashion even before plaque deposition105. Aged wild-type mice 
also have fewer synapses and neurons in the hippocampus than 
do young mice, along with greater anxiety and impaired learn-
ing, features that are ‘abolished’ in mice deficient in the comple-
ment component C3106. Synapses are also lost in a C3-dependent 
manner in mice after infection with an attenuated strain of West 
Nile virus107. Complement components are also more abundant in 
brains from people with HD than in those of age-matched control 
subjects108. It remains to be determined whether increased comple-
ment on synapses in the diseased state results solely from a higher 
concentration of complement or also from increased affinity of 
synapses for complement.
Beyond production of the complement component C1q by 
microglia, another potential association between the induction of 
DAM and complement lies in the link between type I interferon 
responses and synapse loss. Viral overexpression of IFN-β​ in the 
brain impairs cognitive function and causes hippocampal synapse 
loss and is associated with an increase in expression of the comple-
ment component C4b56. In the 564Igi mouse model of systemic 
lupus (generated by the expression of a self-specific B cell antigen 
receptor to drive autoimmunity), cognitive deficits and synap-
tic pruning by microglia are partially dependent on type I inter-
ferons109. Other neurological diseases are known to be caused by 
mutations that specifically result in elevated expression of type 
I interferons. For example, Aicardi-Goutières syndrome can be 
caused by loss-of-function mutations in TREX1 (which encodes 
a cytosolic exonuclease), and pseudo-TORCH syndrome can be 
caused by loss-of-function mutations in USP18 (which encodes a 
ubiquitin-specific protease). The products of these genes each act to 
limit type I interferon responses110–112. We are tempted to speculate 
that these so-called ‘interferonopathies’, which feature gross loss of 
neurons, represent an extreme example of the pruning of synapses 
by overactive DAM.
In addition to pruning synapses, during development, microg-
lia engulf apoptotic neurons via the receptors Tyro3, Axl and Mer, 
which recognize phosphatidylserine. While Mer is expressed on 
microglia during homeostasis, Axl expression is upregulated in 
DAM. Mer-deficient and Axl-deficient mice accumulate apoptotic 
neurons but also have a greater number of live neurons in the 
olfactory bulb, which suggests that microglia also engulf certain 
viable neurons. Mice deficient in both Axl and Mer have a mod-
est increase in survival time in the Thy1-SynhA53Ttg model of PD113. 
Further evidence of the engulfment of live neurons by microglia has 
been provided by live imaging of microglia and neurons in 3xTg 
mice (a combined Aβ​-transgenic–tauopathy model), which has 
revealed that microglia tend to localize together with neurons that 
subsequently disappear, hinting that they had been eliminated by 
those microglia114.
Additional evidence supports the idea of a net negative effect 
of microglia on the integrity of neurons and synapses during dis-
ease. Pharmacological depletion of microglia with PLX3397 or 
with PLX5622, another small-molecule CSF1R antagonist, some-
what restores the performance of 3xTg and 5XFAD mice in vari-
ous behavioral tests92,115 and decreases neuronal and dendritic-spine 
loss in 5XFAD mice. That same treatment reduces memory deficits 
after cranial irradiation116 and motor deficits in SODG93A mice117. 
Analogous observations have been made in experiments with-
out ablation of microglia but with prevention their full activation. 
TREM2 deficiency diminishes hippocampal atrophy and neu-
ronal loss in the P301S model of tauopathy43, while deficiency in 
the TREM2 signaling adaptor DAP12 leads to a reduction in elec-
trophysiological abnormalities and cognitive deficits in APPswe/
PSEN1dE9 mice118. In the model of treatment with MPTP, both 
IFNAR deficiency and IFN-γ​ deficiency produced modest benefi-
cial effects functional outcomes, such as the abundance of dopami-
nergic neurons and behavioral testing, alongside modest decreases 
in microglial activation, as assessed by morphology26,60. Thus, inhib-
iting microglia or specifically DAM can reduce the loss of synapses 
and neurons as well as cognitive decline.
The ability of microglia to exacerbate neurodegeneration has 
also been shown by gain-of-function studies. Microglial deficiency 
in the transmembrane protein NRROS in mice leads to loss of the 
homeostatic microglial signature via impairment of the local avail-
ability of TGF-β​119. Starting at around 2–4 months of age, these mice 
develop a progressive, lethal neurodegenerative disease with motor 
deficits119,120. Direct ablation of TGF-β​ signaling in microglia causes 
similar transcriptional changes and leads to motor deficits within 
2 months, and replacing endogenous microglia in wild-type mice 
with peripheral myeloid cells deficient in TGF-β​ signaling induces 
these changes even faster, within 1 month68. In a different system, 
constitutive activation of the mitogen-activated protein kinase path-
way in microglia through inducible expression of the BRAF V600E 
oncogene led to proliferation and activation of microglia and a 
lethal motor phenotype within 3–4 months121. Thus, strong activa-
tion of microglia can initiate loss of neurons and neurodegeneration 
without other pathological inputs.
Microglia can also indirectly damage neurons by promoting 
neurotoxic astrocyte functions. Astrocyte-specific inhibition of sig-
naling through the pro-inflammatory NF-κ​B pathway ameliorated 
disease in a model of spinal cord injury122. In Aβ​-transgenic mouse 
models, inhibition of the purinergic receptor P2Y1 on astrocytes 
improves brain connectivity and cognitive function independently 
of a decrease in Aβ​123. An in vivo neurotoxic astrocyte signature 
after injection of LPS has been identified and has been shown to 
be dependent on microglia-derived TNF, complement component 
C5a and IL-1α​124. This microglia–astrocyte neurotoxic axis was also 
shown to mediate the effects of α​-synuclein in vitro125. Reactive 
astrocytes are present in AD, HD, ALS and PD, as well as around 
active demyelinating lesions in multiple sclerosis124. However, as 
Nature Immunology | VOL 19 | OCTOBER 2018 | 1048–1058 | www.nature.com/natureimmunology
1052

Review Article
NATure IMMunology
DAM are distinct from LPS-stimulated microglia, it will be impor-
tant to determine the dependence of astrocyte reactivity on microg-
lia in neurodegeneration models.
Neurodegeneration-associated genes expressed 
specifically in microglia
On the basis of genetic studies, and contrary to observations of 
mouse models, microglial function seems to be beneficial, over-
all, in humans in the context of neurodegeneration. Extremely rare 
homozygous loss-of-function mutations in the genes encoding 
TREM2 or DAP12 lead to Nasu-Hakola disease126,127, which is char-
acterized by bone cysts and white-matter neurodegeneration begin-
ning in middle age. A similar neurodegenerative phenotype known 
as ‘hereditary diffuse leukoencephalopathy with spheroids’ is found 
in patients carrying rare heterozygous loss-of-function mutations 
in the gene encoding CSF1R, the receptor for the myeloid-lineage 
growth factor CSF-1128–130. Thus, certain microglia-specific defects 
can drive early-onset neurodegenerative disease. Additional genetic 
links between microglia and AD have been identified that indi-
cate the involvement of microglia as disease modifiers rather than 
bystanders. Common variants of the immune-system molecules 
CR1 (a complement receptor)131, CD33 (an inhibitory receptor)132 
and SHIP-1 (a phosphatase)133 have mild effects on the risk of AD. 
More tellingly, the rare R47H variant of TREM2 has been shown to 
increases the risk of AD by several-fold in many independent stud-
ies134–137, and additional TREM2 variants, such as R62H136,137 and 
H157Y138, also seem to boost the risk of AD. Rare variants of DAP12 
have been associated with early-onset AD139, and a rare protec-
tive variant, P522R, has been identified for the signaling molecule 
PLCG2, which in the brain is expressed predominantly by microglia 
and can signal downstream of DAP12137. Additionally, a common 
protective haplotype is associated with decreased expression of the 
microglial master regulator PU.120.
To better understand whether microglia are beneficial or harm-
ful for AD, investigators have attempted to probe the effects of these 
risk-modifying variants on microglial function. R47H and R62H, 
the most well-established risk variants of TREM2 for AD, are prob-
ably partial loss-of-function mutants, as assessed by a reporter-cell 
assay29,140, direct binding assays53–55, structural analysis141,142 and, in 
the case of R47H, in vivo data from 5XFAD mice143. Brain speci-
mens from human carriers of R47H with AD have fewer microglia, 
less-compact plaques and more dystrophic neurites and tau pathol-
ogy around plaques70 than those from human non-carriers with AD, 
concordant with the phenotypes of TREM2-deficient mice. The

Fig. 3 | Certain genetic alterations that affect mainly microglia or both microglia and neurons are linked to neurodegeneration. a, Certain 
neurodegeneration-associated mutations occur in genes expressed specifically by microglia. Haploinsufficiency of the gene encoding CSF1R causes 
hereditary diffuse leukoencephalopathy with spheroids (HDLS), while homozygous loss of either the gene encoding TREM2 or the gene encoding DAP12 
causes Nasu-Hakola disease (NHD). The R47H and R62H loss-of-function variants of TREM2 increase the risk of late-onset Alzheimer’s disease (LOAD), 
while certain rare variants of DAP12 increase the risk of early-onset Alzheimer’s disease (EOAD). On the other hand, the P522R variant of PLCG2, which 
has undetermined functional consequences, provides protection against LOAD. These molecules are all known to promote the survival and activation 
of microglia. An expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) correlated with decreased expression of the myeloid master transcription factor PU.1 is 
protective against LOAD, although the effects of this decreased expression are unknown. b, Many mutations encoding pathogenic variants that cause 
neurodegeneration and have known neuron-intrinsic mechanisms might also have microglia-intrinsic effects, especially in promoting inflammatory 
responses. These include the huntingtin repeat expansion, mutant SOD1 and progranulin deficiency. Intronic repeat expansions of C9orf72 might decrease 
the expression of C9orf72, and this might lead to decreased lysosomal function in microglia.

fact that R47H and R62H result in a predisposition to AD strongly 
suggests that microglia are protective. The in vivo effects of the 
other aforementioned mutant proteins on microglial function have 
not yet been determined, and it will be intriguing to see whether 
they are also consistent with a protective function for microglia 
in AD (Fig. 3a).
Microglia-intrinsic side effects of other 
neurodegeneration-associated molecules
Other significant risk alleles for AD and other neurodegenerative 
diseases are genes whose expression is not restricted to microglia 
but might affect microglial function (Fig. 3b). The main caveat of 
studying these effects is that the molecules encoded by these genes 
often have strong, direct effects on other cells, which secondarily 
affect microglial phenotypes. In other words, if a risk allele increases 
pathology completely independently of microglia, microglial phe-
notypes would still be altered in response. Even so, such studies 
can suggest possible microglia-intrinsic mechanisms. The Apoe 
risk allele ε​4, which makes the greatest single-locus contribution 
to the genetic risk of late-onset AD144, results in boosted microglial 
activation and worsened pathology in the P301S mouse model of 
AD, and at least some of the neurotoxic effects of Apoe ε​4 can be 
reproduced in vitro through the use of microglia expressing different 
isoforms of ApoE145.
In ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD), neurodegenera-
tive disease–causing mutations might have additional microglia-
intrinsic effects. Mutant SOD1 forms aggregates in motoneurons 
and causes loss of motoneurons42,146. However, overexpression of 
mutant SOD1 exclusively in neurons147 is not sufficient to induce 
loss of motoneurons, and deletion of mutant SOD1 in microglia 
extends the survival of mice148,149. Intronic repeat expansions in 
C9orf72 generate toxic RNA and dipeptide repeats that are suf-
ficient to cause neurodegeneration in mice150–153. The repeats also 
lower the expression of C9orf72, which in homozygosity can cause 
macrophage lysosomal dysfunction154. A rare cause of familial ALS 
and FTD is loss-of-function mutations in TBK1. While TBK1 has 
well-described immunological functions as a kinase, ALS-causing 
mutations do not seem to affect those functions but instead affect 
the binding of optineurin155. Heterozygous loss-of-function muta-
tions in the gene encoding progranulin, a secreted protein with neu-
rotrophic effects156 and effects on lysosomal function157, can cause 
FTD158,159. A decrease in the number of synapses, behavioral changes 
and reduced survival of progranulin-deficient mice160 are par-
tially dependent on complement, which indicates the involvement

Fig. 4 | Possible non–mutually exclusive explanations for discrepancies between human genetics and mouse model experiments. a, Microglia might be 
beneficial in responding to extracellular insults, such as myelin debris or protein aggregates. These are present in AD, Nasu-Hakola disease (NHD) and 
hereditary diffuse leukoencephalopathy with spheroids (HDLS), in which genetic data suggest that microglia are protective. However, microglia might 
be harmful in responding to intraneuronal stresses that occur in other diseases, such as ALS, PD and HD, as well as in AD. Arrows indicate beneficial 
functions (green) or detrimental functions (red). b, Microglia might have a fixed capacity to remove pathological debris. Thus, microglia are proportionally 
more effective in human pathology, with slow deposition, than in mouse models, with rapid deposition. As microglia are overwhelmed, they might begin 
to exert more pro-inflammatory, deleterious functions. c, The risk of developing a human neurodegenerative disease depends on factors that precede 
diagnosis, whereas in mouse models, effects are measured largely after disease onset. Microglia might be beneficial before clinical disease but shift to 
being harmful after disease is established. If this is the case, risk factors and effective therapeutics might actually operate via distinct mechanisms.  
d, Differences in observed roles of microglia in human disease versus those in mouse models might stem from genetically encoded interspecies differences 
in microglial gene expression or the function of the molecules encoded or, alternatively, from differences in environmental exposure. Studies of human 
microglia, either ex vivo or derived from stem cells, will be key to understanding the functions of human microglia.
Nature Immunology | VOL 19 | OCTOBER 2018 | 1048–1058 | www.nature.com/natureimmunology
1054

Review Article
NATure IMMunology
of microglia161. Furthermore, progranulin-deficient macrophages 
produced more inflammatory cytokines in response to LPS stimu-
lation160, and myeloid-selective deletion of progranulin in mouse 
models of treatment with MPTP162 and Aβ​-transgenic mouse mod-
els163 worsens outcomes. These effects could result either from 
microglia-intrinsic changes or from the reduced availability of 
secreted progranulin for neurons.
Other neurodegenerative diseases have less-clear genetic evi-
dence of the involvement of microglia. While an expression quan-
titative trait locus for MHCII has been linked to a 15–30% increase 
in the risk of sporadic PD, indicative of the involvement of adaptive 
immunity, other polymorphisms related to early-onset or sporadic 
PD are not clearly linked to microglia but instead are linked to mito-
chondrial function164. α​-synuclein in PD is analogous to Aβ​ in AD, 
in that aggregates of α​-synuclein are found in sporadic PD165, muta-
tions in the gene encoding α​-synuclein can drive early-onset PD166 
and α​-synuclein can activate microglia in vitro167; however, unlike 
aggregates of Aβ​, aggregates of α​-synuclein are intraneuronal, 
which means that microglia cannot access them until neurons die. 
HD is caused by repeat expansions in the gene encoding hunting-
tin168 that lead to neurotoxic RNA and protein species169. Huntingtin 
is also expressed in myeloid cells, and an exaggerated inflammatory 
cytokine profile has been reported both in peripheral blood and in 
ex vivo–stimulated monocytes from carriers of mutant huntingtin 
well before the predicted onset of disease170. Expression of mutant 
huntingtin specifically in CX3CR1+ microglia leads to upregulation 
of the myeloid-lineage transcription factors PU.1 and C/EBP and 
their target genes encoding the transcription factor IRF1 and TLR2, 
in addition to those encoding IL-6 and TNF. Functionally, microglia 
expressing mutant huntingtin promote neuronal death in vivo in 
the presence of LPS171. Therefore, in HD, as in SOD1-linked ALS, 
expression of the mutant protein in microglia can trigger dysregula-
tion of microglia that causes or contributes to the disease.
In summary, studies have zeroed in on functions that are specific 
to the microglia, such as complement-mediated destruction of syn-
apses, engulfment of live neurons and activation of astrocytes. Like 
macrophages in other tissues, microglia also phagocytose and clear 
extracellular protein aggregates and other debris. With exogenous 
activation, these functions intensify, which in some cases leads to 
enhanced control of pathology but in other cases leads to sponta-
neous neurodegeneration. Several mutations that result in specific 
inhibition of the function of microglia cause or result in a predispo-
sition to neurodegeneration, while certain mutations that encode 
mutant proteins that directly cause neuronal death seem to have 
additional microglia-intrinsic pro-inflammatory effects. Better-
controlled microglia-depletion studies with multiple independent 
approaches, as well as the in vitro and in vivo characterization of 
newly discovered microglia-specific protective and risk alleles, will 
further the understanding of whether microglia are harmful or ben-
eficial in neurodegenerative disease.
Conclusions
The DAM phenotype is present in and has a functional effect on a 
host of neuropathological conditions, as indicated by human genet-
ics and mouse experiments. DAM downregulate the transcriptional 
signature unique to microglia and upregulate interferon-response 
genes, lysosomal genes, and genes encoding lipid-metabolism mol-
ecules and additional surface receptors. In mouse models driven 
by intraneuronal insults, microglia destroy synapses and neurons 
and exert net negative effects, whereas in Aβ​ and cuprizone mod-
els that more prominently feature aggregates of proteins and lip-
ids, microglia help to clear pathological products and exert neutral 
to net positive effects. On the basis of human genetics of genes 
expressed specifically by microglia (TREM2, DAP12 and CSF1R), 
microglia seem to provide protection against neurodegeneration, 
either by supporting tissue homeostasis during the first decades 
of life or by counteracting neuropathological insults when they 
first appear in middle age. The products of neurodegeneration-
associated alleles of other genes, such as ApoE, huntingtin, SOD1 
and progranulin, can have microglia-intrinsic effects overlaid onto 
their primary toxic effects on neurons, and these effects tend to 
be pro-inflammatory and hasten disease. On the whole, across the 
spectrum of mouse models of neuropathology, microglia seem 
to cause more harm than good; on the contrary, human genetic 
data point to an overall positive role for microglia. Perhaps the 
most obvious examples of this conflict are studies in which loss 
of TREM2 or DAP12 markedly improves the outcome in mouse 
models of the very same human diseases in which these molecules 
are known to be protective43,118.
Several non–mutually exclusive principles can be posited that 
address aspects of the discrepancies noted above (Fig.  4). First, 
microglia might be beneficial in clearing extracellular material but 
harmful when responding to intraneuronal stresses. If so, microg-
lia would be helpful for clearing myelin debris and suppressing 
the Aβ​ load but detrimental in the context of tau, mutant SOD1 
and mutant huntingtin, which are predominantly intracellular. 
Second, all mouse models are designed to exhibit accelerated dis-
ease, but mouse microglia might not have proportionally enhanced 
function. In this case, the slower course of neurodegeneration in 
humans might allow microglia to have a much more important 
role than that in mouse models. Third, microglia might be benefi-
cial mainly in delaying the onset of preclinical disease rather than 
in helping after disease onset. In this case, the observed protective 
effects of TREM2, DAP12 and CSF1R might be attributable to pre-
adult life and might be lost or even reversed once pathology begins 
to accumulate.
A fourth explanation, less palatable but perhaps most impor-
tant of all, is that mice and humans might simply have fundamen-
tal differences that shift the natural history of neurodegenerative 
disease in unpredictable ways. Several studies have defined the 
transcriptome of human microglia172–174. Bearing in mind the 
potential human-specific artifacts that result from acquiring and 
processing fresh post-mortem samples, these studies reveal a more 
immunologically active state, with increased expression of comple-
ment components C2 and C3, MHCII, and the DAM-associated 
molecules ApoE and SPP1. Furthermore, human microglia show 
enrichment for the expression of additional AD risk genes encod-
ing SORL1, SPPL2A and APOC1, which have low expression in 
mouse microglia. In vitro, cultured mouse and human microglia 
show many functional differences175. In vivo, microglia in human 
AD have been reported to have a dystrophic morphology rather 
than an activated morphology95,176, which, among other things, 
could emphasize the role of TREM2 in the metabolism of microglia 
rather than in their activation.
Future studies must identify DAM-inducing stimuli, elucidate 
the functions of molecules encoded by DAM-associated genes and 
deconvolute the roles of homeostatic microglia and DAM before 
and during disease. Finally, the effect of microglia-specific protec-
tive and risk alleles on the function of microglia must be elucidated, 
and the human equivalent of DAM must be identified and charac-
terized. Such advances will link findings in mouse models to human 
disease and set the stage for better-informed therapeutic strategies 
targeting microglia in neurodegeneration.

Published online: 24 September 2018

Integrated systems analysis reveals
conserved gene networks underlying
response to spinal cord injury
Jordan W Squair1†, Seth Tigchelaar1, Kyung-Mee Moon2, Jie Liu1,
Wolfram Tetzlaff1, Brian K Kwon1,3, Andrei V Krassioukov1,4,5,
Christopher R West1,6, Leonard J Foster2,7, Michael A Skinnider2†*
1International Collaboration on Repair Discoveries, University of British Columbia,
Vancouver, Canada; 2Centre for High-Throughput Biology, University of British
Columbia, Vancouver, Canada; 3Department of Orthopaedics, University of British
Columbia, Vancouver, Canada; 4GF Strong Rehabilitation Centre, Vancouver Health
Authority, Vancouver, Canada; 5Department of Medicine, Division of Physical
Medicine and Rehabilitation, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, Canada;
6School of Kinesiology, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, Canada;
7Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology and Michael Smith
Laboratories, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, Canada
Abstract Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a devastating neurological condition for which there are
currently no effective treatment options to restore function. A major obstacle to the development
of new therapies is our fragmentary understanding of the coordinated pathophysiological
processes triggered by damage to the human spinal cord. Here, we describe a systems biology
approach to integrate decades of small-scale experiments with unbiased, genome-wide gene
expression from the human spinal cord, revealing a gene regulatory network signature of the
pathophysiological response to SCI. Our integrative analyses converge on an evolutionarily
conserved gene subnetwork enriched for genes associated with the response to SCI by small-scale
experiments, and whose expression is upregulated in a severity-dependent manner following injury
and downregulated in functional recovery. We validate the severity-dependent upregulation of this
subnetwork in rodents in primary transcriptomic and proteomic studies. Our analysis provides
systems-level view of the coordinated molecular processes activated in response to SCI.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.39188.001
Introduction
Spinal cord injury (SCI) results in impairment of motor, sensory, and autonomic systems, causing pro-
found deregulation of almost every bodily function. The failure of large-scale clinical trials of drug
therapies in acute SCI (Bracken et al., 1990; Geisler et al., 2001), and the lack of success in translat-
ing preclinical therapies to humans (Ramer et al., 2014), leaves clinicians without effective treatment
options for SCI. As such, hemodynamic management and surgical decompression remain the only
options to influence neurological outcomes immediately following acute SCI, typically with only mar-
ginal improvements (Hawryluk et al., 2015; Tee et al., 2017; Fehlings et al., 2012). The absence of
an effective treatment for SCI reflects the complexity of the pathophysiologic mechanisms activated
by central nervous system (CNS) injury. The additive effects of the immune response (Kigerl et al.,
2009; Demjen et al., 2004), multiple forms of cell death (Springer et al., 1999; Crowe et al.,
1997), neuronal growth suppression (GrandPre´ et al., 2000; Schnell and Schwab, 1990), and the
Squair et al. eLife 2018;7:e39188. DOI: https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.39188
1 of 23
RESEARCH ARTICLE

formation of an inhibitory glial scar (Bradbury et al., 2002) pose a challenge to the development of
new therapeutic strategies.
A major obstacle to the development of targeted therapies for SCI is the fragmentary state of
our understanding of SCI pathophysiology. The response to trauma within the human spinal cord is
mediated by multiple coordinated molecular pathways, yet these processes are rarely studied in an
integrated manner. An additional challenge in translation of novel therapies is the reliance of clinical
trials
on
standardized
neurological
assessments
for
patient
enrolment
and
stratification
(Fawcett et al., 2007). These measures are highly variable, operator-dependent, and may be impos-
sible to perform in many SCI patients (Kwon et al., 2017). Systems biology approaches provide
powerful means to elucidate the coordinated molecular processes underlying the pathophysiology
of complex diseases (Voineagu et al., 2011; Parikshak et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013;
Johnson et al., 2016). In particular, gene coexpression network analysis can complement reduction-
ist descriptions of isolated gene functions by identifying networks of genes responsible for driving
disease processes (Zhang and Horvath, 2005; Parikshak et al., 2015). Systems-level analyses may
additionally have the potential to suggest novel biomarkers capable of stratifying injury severity and
predicting functional recovery, and consequently to facilitate the translation of new therapies for
acute SCI.
In the present study, we describe an integrated systems biology approach to study the patho-
physiology of SCI. We systematically survey decades of biomedical literature in order to establish
the complete set of genes implicated in the response to SCI by small-scale experiments. We then
integrate this literature-curated gene set with unbiased gene expression data from the human spinal
cord. We use weighted gene coexpression network analysis (WGCNA) to establish the normal bio-
logical processes within the healthy human spinal cord, and conduct a meta-analysis of publicly avail-
able gene expression data to define the gene regulatory network signature of the coordinated
physiological response to SCI. We validate our findings at the transcriptomic and proteomic levels,
and leverage the resulting systems-level understanding of SCI pathophysiology to define candidate
biomarkers for stratification of injury severity and prediction of functional recovery.
Results
Systematic literature analysis identifies genes associated with response
to SCI
Despite decades of study, an integrated understanding of the pathophysiological response to SCI
remains elusive. This gap represents a central challenge to the development of targeted therapies
for SCI. We hypothesized that such an integrated understanding could be achieved by integrating
the vast corpus of SCI literature, collected by small-scale experimentation over several decades,
within an unbiased, genome-wide framework. An overview of our experimental design is shown in
Figure 1.
As a first step, we sought to systematically establish the complete set of genes implicated in the
physiological response to SCI. We conducted a systematic analysis of the SCI literature, reviewing
over 500 papers, in order to reveal a set of 695 unique human genes associated with the response
to SCI by small-scale experiments (Supplementary file 1). Of these genes, 559 were upregulated
following SCI, 213 were downregulated, and the protein products of 8 were differentially phosphory-
lated. Among all genes, 151 were associated with the response to SCI by more than one study
(Figure 2A). The complete set includes genes that have been associated with SCI in a wide range of
experimental models of SCI, in addition to human injuries (Figure 2—figure supplement 1A); in
multiple species, including human as well as rat, mouse, and rabbit (Figure 2—figure supplement
1B); using a range of experimental techniques (Figure 2B); and at a variety of time points, from 1 hr
to 6 months post-injury (Figure 2—figure supplement 1C).
Validation of literature-curated SCI genes
We validated the biological relevance of our literature-curated (LC) SCI gene set using multiple lines
of evidence. First, we established that LC genes were more likely to share common biological func-
tions than random sets of genes, using annotations from the Gene Ontology (Ashburner et al.,
2000). Because functional annotations may be specific or broad, we confirmed that the enrichment
Squair et al. eLife 2018;7:e39188. DOI: https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.39188
2 of 23
Research article
Computational and Systems Biology
Neuroscience

held regardless of the number of genes to which each term was annotated (Figure 2C). Next, we
investigated the tendency for the protein products of LC genes to physically interact. Significant
enrichment for protein-protein interactions (PPIs) between LC genes was observed relative to ran-
dom expectation (Figure 2D, empirical p < 10␀3), and we reproduced this finding in multiple inde-
pendent PPI databases (all p < 10␀3, Figure 2—figure supplement 2A–C). Genes implicated in a
variety of complex diseases by genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have been found to form
distinct modules of densely interacting proteins within the human interactome (Ghiassian et al.,
2015). We therefore evaluated whether this same principle held for SCI by calculating the size of the
largest connected component (LCC) between LC genes, and found that LC genes collectively
formed a significantly larger subnetwork than random expectation (Figure 2E, empirical p < 10␀3), a
finding that was again reproduced in independent interaction datasets (p < 10␀3, Figure 2—figure
supplement 2D–F). Literature-curated genes also displayed a significant tendency to participate in
the same protein complexes (Figure 2—figure supplement 2J). Finally, LC genes were preferentially
recovered by a disease gene prediction algorithm when a subset of them were randomly withheld,

Figure 1. Schematic overview of systems biology approach to SCI pathophysiology integrating small-scale
experiments with high-throughput data. Systematic analysis of over 500 manuscripts revealed the complete set of
genes implicated in SCI pathophysiology by small-scale experiments. SCI genes were integrated with unbiased,
genome-wide gene expression data from healthy human spinal cord to identify coexpressed gene subnetworks
enriched for known SCI genes. Meta-analysis of SCI gene expression data revealed consensus patterns of
subnetwork differential expression after SCI. The resulting consensus network signature of the response to SCI in
human spinal cord was subjected to functional enrichment and cell type analyses, validated at the transcriptomic
and proteomic levels, and leveraged to nominate quantitative biomarkers of SCI severity.

and the remainder used to prioritize additional disease genes (Figure 2F and Figure 2—figure sup-
plement 2G–I). Thus, LC genes represent a biologically relevant and functionally coherent set of
genes, which converge on a common protein interaction module within the human interactome.
Gene coexpression network analysis of human spinal cord
Multiple lines of evidence support the functional coherence of the set of genes implicated in SCI by
small-scale experiments. However, these studies nonetheless have appreciable false positive and
false negative rates, and are limited by sociological and experimental biases. We therefore sought
to integrate knowledge from the SCI corpus within an unbiased, genome-wide framework. We
hypothesized that unsupervised gene coexpression network analysis of human spinal cord would
provide a powerful method to integrate these LC genes in a systems-level context, as this method
has recently been powerfully applied to develop insights into the etiologies of a number of neuro-
logical (Langfelder et al., 2016; Delahaye-Duriez et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2015; Zhang et al.,
2013) or psychiatric diseases (Voineagu et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2013; Fromer et al., 2016).
We constructed gene coexpression networks in human spinal cord using RNA-seq data from 71

scale studies implicating each gene in SCI pathophysiology in the LC gene set. (B) Experimental techniques used to associate LC genes with response
to SCI in the LC gene set. (C) Enrichment for shared Gene Ontology terms among LC genes (all p < 10␀15). BP, biological process; CC, cellular
component; MF, molecular function. (D) Number of protein-protein interactions (PPIs) between LC genes observed in the high-confidence human
interactome (Menche et al., 2015) (dotted line) and 1000 randomized interactome networks (density), revealing significant enrichment for PPIs between
LC genes relative to random expectation (p < 10␀3). (E) Size of the largest connected component (LCC) between LC genes in the high-confidence
human interactome (dotted line) and 1000 randomized interactome networks (density), revealing LC genes occupy a distinct region of the human

(GTEx Consortium, 2013). We applied WGCNA (Langfelder and Horvath, 2008) to group the
human
spinal
cord
transcriptome
into
15
distinct
modules
of
coexpressed
genes
(Supplementary file 2). These modules represent networks of genes that share highly related pat-
terns of expression in the human spinal cord. In order to establish the reproducibility of these spinal
cord gene expression modules in an independent dataset, we constructed a second human spinal
cord gene coexpression network from public microarray data, using established techniques to con-
trol for batch effects (Leek et al., 2012; Vandenbon et al., 2016). Module conservation was quanti-
fied using the Zsummary statistic (Langfelder et al., 2011). Despite the small sample size of our
microarray-based human spinal cord coexpression network (n = 33), seven of 15 modules showed
strong evidence of reproducibility (Zsummary > 10), with an additional two modules showing moderate
evidence of reproducibility (Zsummary > 5) (Figure 3A). Only two of 15 modules showed no evidence
of reproducibility (Zsummary < 2).
Next, we investigated the evolutionary conservation of human spinal cord coexpression modules
in mouse and rat, two of the most commonly used model organisms for studies of SCI pathophysiol-
ogy. We compiled hundreds of microarray samples from mouse (n = 414) and rat (n = 267) spinal
cords from the Gene Expression Omnibus, and constructed gene coexpression networks for the
mouse and rat spinal cords, again using established batch effect correction methods. Five modules
showed strong evidence of evolutionary conservation (Zsummary > 10) in both species, while another
four modules showed moderate evidence of conservation (Zsummary > 5) in at least one species, and

Figure 3. Gene coexpression modules in the human spinal cord and their differential expression in SCI. (A) Reproducibility of human spinal cord
modules in a microarray dataset and conservation in mouse and rat. (B) Enrichment of M3 and M7 for LC SCI genes. (C) Robustness of M3 and M7
enrichment for LC SCI genes. (D) Eigengene network for human spinal cord modules. (E) Differential expression of spinal cord modules following SCI in
five datasets, and consensus. (F) Evidence for differential expression of six consensus modules and one majority module (M8). (G) Time-dependent
expression of spinal cord modules at acute, subacute, and chronic time points following SCI.

only two modules showed no evidence of conservation in either species (Zsummary < 2) (Figure 3A).
Notably, the same five modules that showed the strongest evidence of reproducibility (M2, M3, M7,
M8, and M12) also showed the strongest evidence of conservation in rat and mouse. Thus, at least
at the systems level, the architecture of the spinal cord transcriptome is substantially conserved
between human and model organisms, supporting our approach of integrating data from small-scale
studies of mammalian model organisms.
In order to integrate the LC gene set with the spinal cord coexpression network, we next tested
for enrichment of LC genes within each module (Figure 3B). Two modules, M3 and M7, were signifi-
cantly enriched for LC genes (Fisher’s exact test, Bonferroni-corrected p = 9.5  10␀8 and 2.7 
10␀3, respectively). These modules consist of 746 and 330 genes, respectively, and both are among
the most reproducible and conserved in the spinal cord (Figure 3A). We confirmed the robustness
of the observed enrichment by randomly removing seed genes from the LC set, and by randomly
adding false positive genes to the LC set. Both M3 and M7 remained significantly enriched for LC
genes despite the removal of a large number of seed genes, or the addition of a large number of
random genes (Figure 3C): M3 remained significantly enriched for LC genes even after the removal
of approximately 70% of genes from the seed set, compared to approximately 50% for M7. More-
over, M3 remained significantly enriched for seed genes even after the size of the literature-curated
set was doubled by addition of random false positives. We also asked whether the observed enrich-
ment was driven most strongly by any individual analytical technique or injury model, but found the
majority of experimental methods, SCI models, and species contributed to the observed LC gene
enrichment in M3 and M7 (Figure 3—figure supplement 1). Thus, M3 and M7 are robustly enriched
for genes associated to the SCI response by small-scale studies, despite their divergent experimental
designs.
Finally, to assess the relationships between modules, we constructed a module meta-network
based on the eigengene of each module, defined as the first principal component of module expres-
sion (Langfelder and Horvath, 2007) (Figure 3D). In the resulting network, M3 and M7 clustered
together, as would be expected given the strong correlation between their eigengenes (Spearman’s
 = 0.54, p = 1.6  10␀6). These results suggest that the expression of these two modules in the spi-
nal cord is highly correlated.
In summary, gene coexpression network analysis identified five highly conserved and reproduc-
ible modules, two of which are significantly and robustly enriched for LC genes, and whose expres-
sion is highly correlated.
Meta-analysis of coexpression network deregulation in SCI
We next characterized the role of M3 and M7, as well as other highly conserved coexpression mod-
ules, in the pathophysiological response to SCI. We performed a meta-analysis of five mouse and rat
transcriptomic studies of SCI within the context of our spinal cord coexpression network, in order to
identify consensus changes in the spinal cord transcriptome at the module level in response to SCI
(Figure 3E). This analysis identified M3, M6, M7, and M11 as consensus upregulated, and M1 and
M2 as consensus downregulated, following SCI. One other module, M8, was upregulated following
SCI in four of five datasets, while the remaining eight modules did not show robust evidence of dif-
ferential expression. Among all seven modules, M2, M3, and M7 consistently showed the strongest
evidence of differential expression (Figure 3F, p  6.5  10␀36, 1.2  10␀48, and 1.6  10␀14,
respectively). Notably, among these modules, M2, M3, M7 were strongly conserved and reproduc-
ible in mouse, rat, and human networks (Zsummary > 10), whereas M1, M6, and M11 displayed only
moderate evidence of conservation (2 < Zsummary < 10), suggesting these modules may capture
human-specific aspects of spinal cord transcriptome organization that are relevant in the response to
SCI.
Because the pathophysiological processes underlying primary and secondary injury in SCI are
incompletely understood, we additionally investigated the expression of spinal cord modules at
acute, subacute, and chronic time points. Consensus module expression was remarkably consistent
at all time points studied (Figure 3G). However, analysis of the temporal regulation of spinal cord
modules revealed consensus downregulation of M9 at the most acute time point after SCI, but con-
sensus upregulation at a chronic time point. These results suggest M9 may be specifically involved in
the transition between acute and chronic physiological responses following SCI. Thus, by integrating
Squair et al. eLife 2018;7:e39188. DOI: https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.39188
6 of 23
Research article
Computational and Systems Biology
Neuroscience

gene coexpression network analysis with a meta-analysis of the SCI transcriptome, we reveal a con-
sensus network signature associated with the response to SCI, and a network module specifically
implicated in the transition from acute to chronic injury processes.
Functional characterization of consensus signature modules
We sought to characterize the biological significance of the modules implicated in the physiological
response to SCI by integrating functional annotations from the Gene Ontology (Ashburner et al.,
2000) and molecular signatures from MSigDB (Liberzon et al., 2011) (Supplementary file 3). To
visualize statistically overrepresented gene sets, we constructed enrichment maps for each consen-
sus signature module (Merico et al., 2010) (Figure 4A–B and Figure 4—figure supplements 1–
4). To appreciate the cell type-specificity of each module, we additionally conducted a meta-analysis
of transcriptomic and proteomic profiles from the major cell types of the CNS, incorporating both
bulk and single-cell RNA-seq datasets (Zhang et al., 2014; Sharma et al., 2015; Cahoy et al., 2008;
Zeisel et al., 2018) (Figure 4C and Figure 4—figure supplement 5). M1 was an oligodendrocyte
module, associated with axon ensheathment and myelination, whereas M2 was a neuronal module
implicated in synaptic transmission. M3 was enriched for markers of microglia and vascular endothe-
lial cells, and biological processes such as inflammatory response and response to wounding, while
M7 was a microglial module enriched for annotations related to the immune response. M9 was
enriched for astrocyte markers and terms such as oxidation-reduction process, as well as the term
central nervous system development, which may be related to its upregulation at chronic time points
following SCI. M6 and M11 were not significantly associated with any specific cell type, and were
enriched for terms including cellular protein modification process and mitochondrial translation,
respectively.
Network analysis of SCI severity and recovery
The finding that M3 is a highly conserved and reproducible gene coexpression module, with the
most significant enrichment for LC genes and strong evidence of upregulation following SCI, sug-
gested that this module plays a key pathophysiological role in SCI. We focused on the role of M3 in
SCI by investigating the relationship between M3 expression and two key clinical parameters in SCI:
injury severity and recovery of sensory and motor function.
We first re-analysed gene expression data from a mouse model of severity-dependent injury to
identify relationships between consensus module expression and injury severity (Di Giovanni et al.,
2003; De Biase et al., 2005). Strikingly, M3 was the sole module enriched for genes positively corre-
lated to injury severity, whereas M1, M2, and M9 were enriched for genes anti-correlated to injury
severity (Figure 5A). We investigated this effect further by considering the correlations between
module eigengenes, which provide a summary of the expression profile of each module, and injury
severity. This analysis revealed that the M3 eigengene was the most strongly correlated with injury
severity (Spearman’s  = 0.79, p = 2.5  10␀7), with a clear separation in M3 expression between
the mild, severe, and sham injury groups at 7 days post-injury (Figure 5E).
In order to validate the severity-dependent upregulation of M3 following SCI, we conducted a
prospective experimental SCI study, using the field standard contusion injury model at the T10 seg-
ment, and performed RNA sequencing of the spinal cord parenchyma in rats subjected to moderate,
severe, or sham injuries (n = 5 per group). Our RNA-seq data reproduced the consensus network
signature derived from our meta-analysis of microarray datasets, emphasizing the robustness of this
systems-level characterization of SCI pathophysiology (Figure 5F). In addition, we confirmed the sig-
nificant association between injury severity and the M3 eigengene (Figure 5E; Spearman’s  = 0.94,
p = 4.2  10␀7). Thus, insights into the network-level organization of the transcriptome in SCI
derived from a meta-analysis of publicly available data replicate in an independently collected
dataset.
Together, these results emphasized the severity-dependent upregulation of M3 following SCI,
and suggested that the expression of a gene or combination of genes that accurately summarize the
transcriptional status of M3 has the potential to serve as an objective biomarker of SCI severity. To
evaluate the potential of such an indicator as a biomarker of injury severity, we focused on the hub
genes of M3. These genes are the most central and interconnected within the module, based on
their correlation to the module eigengene, and are highly enriched for functionally relevant genes

cell type-specific marker gene enrichment in human spinal cord modules at the transcriptomic and proteomic levels.
