1Department of Dermatology, Venereology and Allergology, Leipzig University Medical Faculty, Leipzig, Germany. 2Department of Microbiology, Tumor 
and Cell Biology, Karolinska Institute, Stockholm, Sweden. 3Department for Plastic Surgery and Hand Surgery, Klinikum Rechts der Isar, School of 
Medicine, Technical University of Munich, Munich, Germany. 4Chinese Institutes for Medical Research, Beijing, China. 5Capital Medical University, Beijing, 
China. 6These authors contributed equally: Sandra Franz, Yuval Rinkevich. 
 e-mail: sandra.franz@medizin.uni-leipzig.de; yuval.rinkevich@cimrbj.ac.cn

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
721
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
Fibroblasts are traditionally viewed as mesenchymal cells; however, 
emerging evidence indicates that fibroblasts arise from diverse 
embryonic origins, leading to functional specialization across differ-
ent tissues21,22. Fibroblasts arise from two primary embryonic germ 
layers: the ectoderm (neural crest cells) and mesoderm (paraxial and 
lateral plate mesoderm; Box 1)23,24 (Fig. 1).
Progenitors in the skin and heart derive from the ectoderm and 
mesoderm, whereas fibroblasts in other organs have mesodermal 
origins23–27.
Developmental genes, particularly the homeobox (HOX) family 
(Box 1), play a central role in cell fate specification, as well as spatiotem-
poral pattern formation during embryogenesis. In humans, HOX genes 
guide the cell fate restriction and location-specific differentiation of 
fibroblasts at the embryonic stage28,29. Remarkably, even in adult life, 
fibroblasts retain a specific set of HOX genes, reflecting their embryonic 
origin as found in fibroblasts from facial and body skin30. Furthermore, 
epigenetic modifications of HOX genes contribute to the positional 
memory of fibroblasts, which is maintained even after isolation from 
their tissue of origin, persisting over several passages31. HOX genes 
encode a conserved group of transcription factors that translate posi-
tional identity along the anterior–posterior and secondary axes into 
origin-dependent cell functions. This suggests that the embryonic ori-
gin of fibroblasts, as determined by their HOX gene expression patterns, 
may also influence their specialized functions in adult tissues22,29,32.
patterns or pathogen-associated molecular patterns via pattern rec-
ognition receptors. In response, they can produce antimicrobial and 
host defence peptides, attract immune cells to the sites of inflamma-
tion or injury and exhibit immune regulatory functions in innate and 
adaptive immune responses10–12. In addition, fibroblast plasticity can 
be influenced by factors from the tissue itself, such as mechanical stress 
and tissue stiffness. This can be seen, for example, when fibroblasts are 
placed in a stiff microenvironment where they are more likely to turn 
into ECM-producing transient myofibroblasts13. Furthermore, fibro-
blasts also exhibit the ability to de-differentiate, function as progenitor 
cells14,15 or enter a senescence program in response to transcription 
factors or growth factor repression16. This plasticity highlights their 
role in tissue repair and regeneration; nonetheless, they can also par-
ticipate in pathological processes, such as chronic wound healing17 
and fibrosis18–20 (Box 1). In this Review, we aim to explore fibroblast 
diversity, particularly from humans, by summarizing the current state 
of research on fibroblast origin, development and functions in diverse 
organs in healthy and disease contexts.
Embryonic origin of the different fibroblast 
populations
The genealogical hierarchy (Box 1) of fibroblast origin during embry-
ogenesis is crucial for clarifying the anatomical distribution, gene 
signatures and functions of fibroblasts in adult tissues and organs. 
BOX 1
Glossary
Buccal cavity. This comprises the inner lining of the cheeks, gums 
and floor of the mouth.
Cephalic mesoderm. This refers to the mesoderm that forms in the 
head region of the embryo. The cephalic mesoderm gives rise to 
fibroblasts that will inhabit the head and face.
Fascia. This layer of the skin is a dense, fibrous connective tissue 
matrix comprising fibroblasts, lymphatics, adipose tissue, vessels 
and sensory neurons. The fascia is pivotal in structural support, 
compartmentalization and mechanotransduction. It is located 
beneath the dermal white adipose tissue and can be distinguished in 
the superficial fascia and deep fascia.
Fibrosis. This is a pathological process occurring as a result 
of chronic injury, inflammation or aberrant wound healing, 
characterized by excessive ECM deposition leading to organ 
dysfunction and failure, scarring and even cancer.
Fibrocytes. This term was initially used to describe cells within 
connective tissue that appeared to have fibroblast characteristics. 
These cells have a haematopoietic origin and are considered to 
be part of the monocyte/macrophage lineage. Fibrocytes express 
both leukocyte (CD45) and mesenchymal markers (collagen) and 
can circulate in the bloodstream, enter tissues and differentiate into 
fibroblast-like cells.
Fibrotic fibroblasts. These are a specialized type of fibroblast 
involved in pathological tissue remodelling and scar formation, 
leading to fibrosis. When fibroblasts become fibrotic, they contribute 
to abnormally or excessively scar-like tissue formation.
Genealogical hierarchy. This describes the structured family tree  
of cells, tracing their lineage and categorization of progenitor cells 
and their differentiated descendants during embryonic  
development.
HOX genes. These are a cluster of genes that encode homeobox 
transcription factors. They play a crucial role in determining the 
positional information, orientation and patterning of tissues during 
embryonic development.
Glabrous skin. This includes the palms, soles and volar surfaces 
of the fingers and toes, which are unique due to their lack of hair 
follicles.
Lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm develops on 
either side of the midline of the embryo and is divided into two layers: 
the somatic mesoderm and splanchnic mesoderm. It contributes to 
the formation of limbs, connective tissues and fibroblasts distributed 
in ventral skin, and various organs (that is, the lungs and heart).
Papillary dermis. This is the superficial layer of the dermis, lying 
just below the epidermal basement membrane. It comprises loose 
connective tissue with fine collagen type III fibres, elastin and a rich 
supply of capillaries, lymphatic vessels and sensory nerve endings. 
This layer is characterized by dermal papillae, which interdigitate with 
the epidermal ridges.
Paraxial mesoderm. The paraxial mesoderm is located adjacent 
to the midline of the embryo. It undergoes segmentation to form 
somites and then differentiates into multiple structures and dermal 
fibroblasts populating dorsal skin.
Reticular dermis. This is the deeper and thicker portion of the dermis, 
extending from the papillary dermis to the subcutaneous tissue. It 
comprises dense irregular connective tissue with thicker bundles 
of collagen type I fibres and a less dense network of blood vessels 
compared with the papillary dermis. The collagen fibres are organized 
in a mesh-like pattern, providing tensile strength and elasticity.
Space of Disse. This represents a small gap between the sinusoidal 
endothelial cells and the hepatocytes in the liver where the HSCs are 
found.
Transdifferentiation. This term refers to the process where one 
differentiated cell type directly transforms into another differentiated 
cell type without going through a pluripotent stem cell stage.

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
722
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
Recent multi-omics analyses, such as single-cell RNA sequencing 
(scRNA-seq) and single-cell assay for transposase-accessible chromatin 
with sequencing (ATAC-seq), reveal that epigenetic mechanisms, par-
ticularly those affecting chromatin accessibility, further shape fibro-
blast lineage commitment. Studies in mouse skin have demonstrated 
that histone modifications such as H3K27me3 that are established 
during early embryogenesis, influence chromatin accessibility and 
contribute to fibroblast fate decisions33,34. These epigenetic changes 
are fundamental for establishing the cell fate of adult fibroblasts.
During embryonic development, different signalling net-
works control the development of specific progenitor fibroblasts 
in organ niches. Some examples include wingless-related integra-
tion (Wnt) signalling, involving Wnt ligands, β-catenin and lymphoid 
enhancer-binding factor 1 (LEF1)32,35, and transforming growth factor-β 
(TGFβ) signalling, which involves TGFβ family proteins such as bone 
morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) and suppressor of mothers against 
decapentaplegic (SMAD) proteins36. In the next section, we will 
explore the diversity of fibroblasts in the different organs and dis-
cuss key insights into their signatures and functions concerning their 
organ-specific environment and signalling.
Diversity of adult fibroblasts
In adult humans, fibroblasts exhibit remarkable diversity between tis-
sues (intersite) and within the same tissue (intrasite). This diversity is 
reflected in their distinct transcriptional profiles and specialized func-
tions. Fibroblast identity and behaviour are influenced by two major 
factors: their embryonic lineage and signals from the tissue-specific 
niche, which work together to maintain organ homeostasis. However, 
the mechanisms by which these lineage programs and niche interac-
tions shape fibroblast function remain only partially understood. Trans-
plantation experiments in mice reveal that the fibroblast signature 
memory-based lineage, rather than tissue signals, primarily determines 
their niche-specific architecture and functions, with intrinsic programs 
driving diversity across sites. Whether this applies to fibroblasts in 
other tissues or human contexts remains unclear.
Various fibroblast subtypes across human tissues have been 
defined by scRNA-seq analyses (Table 1). However, differences in 
sequencing techniques, sequencing depths and analytical approaches 
make it challenging to recognize common fibroblast identities 
between individual studies. A recent study involving single-cell analy-
ses of up to 11 human tissue types in healthy and diseased states cat-
egorized fibroblasts as either tissue specific or universal, depending 
on whether they were present in one or two tissue types or shared 
among most tissues37. Tissue-specific fibroblasts are characterized by 
unique gene signatures tailored to their niches, which support tissue 
maintenance during disease states. By contrast, so-called universal 
fibroblasts (expressing the markers PI16 and COL15A1) are proposed 
to exist across multiple tissues in mice and, to a lesser extent, in 
humans1,37. In mice, universal fibroblasts have been described under 
normal and disease conditions, whereas in humans they have been 
identified only under healthy conditions1,37. It has been suggested 
that in response to metabolic, inflammatory and repair signals in 
the microenvironmental niche, universal fibroblasts may act as 
progenitor-like cells, giving rise to specialized populations, such as 
myofibroblasts in cancer37. However, this raises the question of the 
origin of universal subtypes because fibroblasts in different tissues 
do not necessarily share a common progenitor, but instead follow 
unique developmental pathways. Ultimately, understanding fibro-
blast diversity requires further research to disentangle the interplay 
of lineage origins, niche signals and functional adaptations. Below, 
we summarize the current knowledge on human fibroblast diversity 
in homeostatic and diseased tissues.

development. a,b, Fibroblast progenitor cells primarily derive from the neural 
crest (NC) of the ectoderm (a) and different plates (cephalic, paraxial and lateral) 
of the mesoderm (b). Progenitors from the neuronal crest emerge via epithelial-
to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) mediated by the transcription factor SNAI2 
in humans and Snai1 in mice. Skin fibroblasts have different embryonic origins, 
depending on the location of the skin in the body. From the neuronal crest and 
cephalic mesoderm (CM) arise facial or cranial skin progenitors, whereas the 
paraxial mesoderm (PM) generates dorsal skin progenitors and the lateral plate 
mesoderm (LM) forms ventral skin progenitors. Cardiac fibroblast progenitors 
also derive from the ectodermal neuronal crest and paraxial mesoderm. 
Fibroblast progenitors from all other internal organs, including the lung, liver 
and gut, have mesodermal origin. ENFs, engrailed 1 lineage-negative fibroblasts;

Fibroblast subtypes in homeostatic tissues
Different tissues in a steady state harbour different tissue-specific 
fibroblast subtypes. Every tissue provides distinct structural niches 
that specialize fibroblasts for tissue-specific homeostatic functions, 
which then drive fibroblast heterogeneity within and across tissues. 
Although specific niches occur in all or several tissues (for example, all 
tissues provide a vascular niche, and tissue with an outer epithelial bar-
rier such as lung, gut or skin provide a sub-epithelial niche), fibroblast 
subtypes in these niches may exert similar functions, but they differ in 
respect of their signatures.
Skin
The skin is a complex organ, organized into multiple layers that house 
distinct fibroblast subtypes, each residing in specific niches. These 
layers include the epidermis, the papillary and reticular dermis (Box 1) 
and the hypodermis (also known as dermal white adipose tissue). In 
humans, unlike mice, due to the rudimentary panniculus carnosus, the 
hypodermis is in contact with subcutaneous fat38. Beneath these layers 
lies the superficial fascia (Box 1), a connective tissue that separates the 
skin from the underlying muscles. Apart from the epidermis, each skin 
layer contains distinct fibroblast subtypes (Fig. 2a), exhibiting remark-
able functional diversity driven by their lineage origins22,29,39. In mice, 
fibroblast subsets have been traced back to progenitor markers, such as 
engrailed 1 (En1), Dlk1, Prrx1 and Wnt1, originating from different germ 
layers2,23,40 (Fig. 1). Although the existence of similar lineage-specific 
progenitor markers in humans has to be determined, distinct fibroblast 
populations with unique functions have been identified in specific loca-
tions within human skin. For example, glabrous skin (Box 1) on the palms 
and soles contains a unique population of ANGPTL7-expressing and 
PRG4-expressing (ANGPTL7+PRG4+) palmoplantar fibroblasts, which are 
involved in ECM homeostasis and exhibit enhanced insulin-like growth 
factor (IGF) signalling to support keratinocyte growth in the thickened 
epidermis of these regions41. Additionally, the skin of the oral cavity 
contains fibroblast subpopulations transcriptionally and functionally 
distinct from normal skin, whose functions are tissue remodelling and 
promoting proliferation, reflecting the regenerative potential of oral 
skin (Box 2)42. Other oral fibroblast subsets have an immunoregulatory 
profile with gene signatures typical for the control of leucocyte pro-
liferation (ANXA1 and IGFBP2), granulocyte migration (CXCL1, CXCL2, 
CXCL8 and CCL2) and complement activation (C3 and CFD)43. Further-
more, evidence is accumulating that human fibroblasts in ventral and 
limb skin have distinct identities and can be divided into papillary, 
reticular and fascia lineages, although these vary in numbers and sig-
nature profiles between studies3,30,44–48. Nonetheless, some studies 
lack spatial information on the populations, which makes assignment 
to papillary or reticular lineages more difficult (see Table 1 for an over-
view). Markers of the papillary signature include the Wnt pathway 
inhibitory genes APCDD1, AXIN2 and WIF1 and the epidermal–dermal 
junction collagen genes COL6A1 and COL13A1, as well as COLEC12, pros-
taglandin D2 synthase (PTGDS), podoplanin (PDPN) and decorin (DCN), 
which play a role in host defence, cell adhesion/migration and matrix 
assembly. Papillary fibroblasts’ functions include ECM remodelling, 
collagen catabolic processes and collagen fibril organization3,30,45,48. 
For instance, CD90+CD39+ papillary fibroblasts expressing COL6A1, 
WIF1 and AXIN2 are specialized in producing loose collagen bundles, 
particularly collagen VI, which support the overlying epidermis by 
maintaining the keratinocyte structure3. These fibroblasts also promote 
epidermal growth and stratification, as demonstrated by their ability 
to sustain keratinocyte proliferation when implanted in a decellular-
ized human dermis. Mechanistically, their function is supported by a 
synergistic Wnt-mediated interaction with basal keratinocytes, high-
lighting the importance of fibroblast–keratinocyte crosstalk3. These 
fibroblasts also possess immunomodulatory properties, which may 
indicate their ability to respond to injuries3. Additionally, a subset of 
papillary fibroblasts negative for CD90 but expressing fibroblast acti-
vation protein (FAP) on their surface have been described as a highly 
proliferative population45. By contrast, other studies have suggested 
that FAP is a marker for activated fibroblasts that contribute to disease 
but are absent under healthy conditions20,42. Further investigation 
would be needed to show the intrinsic nature of these cells. Fibroblasts 
with papillary signatures (ASPN and POSTN), including CRABP1+TNN+ 
dermal papilla fibroblasts, are present in the hair follicle niche30,46,48. 
They reside in distinct areas of the hair bulb and infundibulum and 
contribute via reciprocal cell–cell communication to ECM remodelling 
and collagen fibril organization to maintain the hair follicle niche30,48.
The most abundant genes that characterize the reticular fibroblast 
signature include MFAP5, MGP, FGF7, COL11A1, COMP and WISP2. The 
diverse functional roles of these markers reflect the heterogeneity of 
the reticular fibroblast subsets that distribute in the reticular layer 
down to the hypodermis. Fibroblasts enriched in the subcutaneous 
layer are involved in fat storage, fat metabolism and adipogenesis, 
underlining their importance for metabolic regulation beyond purely 
structural functions. For example, reticular CD90+FAP− fibroblasts 
expressing CD36, PPARG and adiponectin (ADIPOQ) can promote 
adipogenesis45, and fibroblasts expressing COL11A1 and RPB4 have 
been suggested to regulate adipocyte progenitor differentiation46. 
CD26+ (DPP4) and secreted frizzled-related protein 2-positive (SFRP2+) 
fibroblasts represent a peculiar population of fibroblasts distributed

Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
across the papillary and reticular layer that are involved in ECM homeo-
stasis and negative regulation of signalling pathways in healthy skin 
and play a major role in skin fibrotic diseases3,41,46. Another group of 
fibroblasts are the perivascular fibroblasts associated with blood ves-
sels in the skin and other organs. These cells express APOD and APOE; 
they contribute to angiogenesis, exhibit inflammatory functions and 
regulate cell migration and adhesion after an injury45,48,49. At the same 
time, they display no ECM-related functions30,45,48. In the fascia, we have 
identified a CD201+ progenitor fibroblast lineage in mice that is pivotal 
for wound healing progression. This population in humans overlaps 
with PI16+ fibroblasts expressing FBLN1, DCN and WISP2 (ref. 39).
Lung
The lungs are the central organs of the respiratory system, comprising 
the bronchus and bronchiole branches that terminate into the alveoli 
where gas diffusion occurs. Recent research has identified several 
fibroblast subtypes in human lungs as genotypically and functionally 
distinct (Table 1) and all reside in distinct niches in the lung (Fig. 2a). 
In the distal lung, which contains the terminal bronchioles and alveoli 
that facilitate gas exchange, fibroblasts are primarily located within 
the thicker regions of the interstitial ECM, positioned between the 
basement membrane of alveolar cells and the endothelial cells50. In 
the airways, fibroblasts (peri-bronchial, adventitial and alveolar) and 
myofibroblasts are found within the connective tissue surrounding 
broncho-vascular bundles and beneath the epithelium, where these 
cells play a crucial role in the formation and maintenance of the fibrous 
ECM system, supporting the structural integrity of lung tissues50–52. 
Adventitial fibroblasts marked by MFAP5+, SFRP2+, PI16+ and serpin fam-
ily F member 1 (SERPINF1) are found around blood vessels near artery 
SMCs along the proximal and distal airways51,53,54. These fibroblasts are 
critical in regulating vascular function, with SERPINF1 contributing to 
vascular remodelling and COL1A2 supporting vessel integrity51,53. These 
cells also express inflammatory genes and genes of the complement 
system, suggesting their involvement in immunoregulation53. In addi-
tion, adventitial fibroblasts, also called perivascular fibroblasts, express 
CD34 and may act as myofibroblast progenitors in diseased lungs54. 
SPINT2+, FGFR4+ and GPC3+ alveolar fibroblasts localize in the alveoli in 
close association with the alveolar epithelial cells53,54. SPINT2 is a serine 
peptidase inhibitor with antiviral functions against several influenza 
viruses (for example, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2) 
and regulates serine proteases involved in cell migration and invasion 
of tumour cells, indicating a role of alveolar fibroblasts in maintaining 
the immunological barrier against pathogens and the integrity of the 
alveolar epithelium55,56. Moreover, alveolar fibroblasts express SCN7A 
sodium channels and GRIA1 glutamate receptors, enabling them to 
respond to glutamatergic signals53. Interestingly, glutamate signal-
ling has been shown to be involved in asthma and lung inflammation57. 
Peri-bronchial fibroblasts, located at the end of the trachea, are the 
most recently identified and unexplored population of fibroblasts in the 
lung. Only a small fraction of these COL15A1+ENTPD1+ cells were found 
in healthy lungs, which substantially expand in pulmonary diseases52. 
The LGR5+ fibroblast population, identified in the sub-epithelial mes-
enchyme of the airways may overlap with the peri-bronchial fibroblast 
population52,58. The LGR5+ cells produce fibroblast growth factor (FGF), 
BMP and WNT ligands and represent a niche for distal airway epithelial 
cells58. WIF1+FGF18+ASPN+ myofibroblasts express COL10A1, CTHRC1 
and actin-α 2 (ACTA2); these cells are distributed within the alveolar 
niche where they are involved in collagen and ECM synthesis essential 
for maintaining alveolar structure52–54. An additional population of 
myofibroblasts are the fibromyocytes, which express MH11, TAGLN and 
CNN1, genes promoting contractility52,53,59. Additionally, in the airways, 
another rare CCL19-expressing and CCL21-expressing fibroblast popula-
tion, with a probable role in immune cell recruitment, has been discov-
ered recently52. Interestingly, the gene signature of these fibroblasts 
maps to lymphoid organ stromal cells, suggesting their involvement in 
response to injury, inflammation and infections. Lastly, PLIN2+APOE+ 
lipofibroblasts, initially described only in mice, have also been identi-
fied in the alveolar interstitial space of humans53,60. These cells, which 
arise from FGF10-expressing mesenchymal progenitor cells, are packed 
with lipid droplets, resembling adipocytes61. Lipofibroblasts synthesize 
triglyceride, which alveolar cells need for the production of surfactant, 
a phospholipid–protein complex that reduces surface tension at the 
air–liquid interface, thus maintaining lung function53,62.
Heart
The heart’s cardiac fibroblasts are found in the right and left atrium 
and ventricle (including the apex), interventricular septum, cardiac 
valves and perivascular region (Fig. 2a) and represent more than 50% 
of all heart cells63,64. A proteomic map of the human heart has identi-
fied several markers specific to cardiac fibroblasts. These include the 
transcription factors GATA4 and ACVR1, which are needed for heart 
development, NPR3, which plays a part in vasodilation, and the newly 
identified receptor tyrosine kinase-like orphan receptor 1 (ROR1)64. 
The heart is one of the first organs to form during embryogenesis, 
and the emergence and differentiation of cardiac fibroblasts are 
intricately linked to the developmental phases of the heart. Studies 
on mammalian hearts, including those of humans, show that cardiac 
fibroblasts originate from four main sources. The epicardium is the 
primary contributor, followed by the endocardium, which supplies 
fibroblasts to the interventricular septum and right ventricle. A smaller 
proportion arises from the neural crest populating the right atrium 
and the dorsal mesenchymal protrusion. Additionally, these areas 
will give rise to cardiac fibroblasts colonizing the valves and coronary 
vasculature65–67. Interestingly, although fibroblasts from diverse origins 
are morphologically similar, they exhibit regional specializations. This 
suggests that not-yet-identified, specific niche signals shape fibro-
blast identities during development. This mechanism persists in the 
adult heart, adapting fibroblast functions to distinct mechanical and 
structural requirements of the various heart regions. In particular, tis-
sue stiffness, collagen matrix composition and density in the atria are 
different from the ventricles, suggesting that fibroblasts in these two 
areas have different ECM functions64. scRNA-seq of the adult human 
Fig. 2 | Stromal cells in health and disease. a, Spatial distributions and locations 
of fibroblast populations in homeostatic organs. (1) Skin. Fibroblasts are 
distributed across dermal layers: the papillary dermis (pDermis), reticular 
dermis (rDermis), dermal white adipose tissue (dWAT) and subcutaneous fascia 
(sFascia). (2) Heart. Cardiac fibroblasts support cardiomyocytes and mediate 
ECM communication. These fibroblasts are distributed in the atrium, ventricles, 
valves and nearby vessels. (3) Lungs. Fibroblasts are found in the upper airways 
(i; peri-bronchial region), lower airways (ii; fibromyocytes, myofibroblasts 
and adventitia) and alveoli (iii; lipofibroblasts and myofibroblasts). (4) Liver. 
Fibroblasts localize around the portal vein and bile duct alongside HSCs. (5) 
Colon. SOX6+ sub-epithelial fibroblasts promote epithelial cell and IESC renewal 
via Wnt5a signalling. Stromal fibroblasts (OGN+) and myofibroblasts synthesize 
and remodel the ECM using BMPs. Crypt fibroblasts (WNT2B) facilitate 
intestinal stem cell proliferation in the stem cell (SC) niche (circular arrows). 
Fibroblast interactions with multipotent tissue-associated cells (TACs) promote 
TAC proliferation. b, Activation states of fibroblasts under non-homeostatic 
conditions. The main functions and example markers of pro-regenerative 
fibroblasts, inflammatory fibroblasts, fibrotic fibroblasts and myofibroblasts 
are depicted here. CD, Crohn’s disease; e, epidermis; EndMT, endothelial-to-
mesenchymal transition; Ent, enterocytes; HF, hair follicle; HPs, hepatocytes;

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
727
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
heart has revealed chamber-specific fibroblast subtypes in the atria 
and ventricles (Table 1), with distinct expression of collagen isoforms 
and other ECM- or connective tissue-related genes68. On the one hand, 
influenced by TGFβ signalling, atrial fibroblasts in the sub-epicardial 
and endocardial layers produce and assemble collagen types I and III 
and other ECM components that contribute to the atrial architecture 
essential for regulating blood flow between the venous system and 
ventricles68. On the other hand, ventricular fibroblasts located in the 
myocardium play a crucial role in synthesizing, remodelling and organ-
izing an ECM with dense collagen fibres to help the heart withstand 
the mechanical forces generated during contraction that are essential 
for pumping blood out to the rest of the body63,68. A further fibroblast 
subtype surrounding the medial layer of the coronary arteries is the 
adventitial fibroblast. Characterization of these cells in the human 
heart is sparse, whereas studies in mice reported Sonic Hedgehog (Shh) 
signalling gene signatures in these cells and the production of a dense 
collagen matrix supporting the artery structure69. A recent study has 
emphasized the significant role of myocardial fibroblasts in crosstalk 
with other cell types in the heart, such as cardiomyocytes and endothe-
lial cells, through ECM-mediated signalling pathways63.
Liver
The liver plays a vital role in metabolizing carbohydrates, lipids and 
proteins, maintaining blood glucose levels and detoxifying harmful 
substances. Additionally, it contributes to immune defence by protect-
ing the body against pathogens and foreign particles in the bloodstream. 
These two major tasks of the liver—metabolism and immune defence—
are also reflected in the function of liver fibroblasts. A recent scRNA-seq 
study on the foetal human liver identified three key mesoderm-derived 
cell populations originating from the hepatic mesenchyme. Hepatic 
stellate cells (HSCs), portal/peri-portal fibroblasts and vascular SMCs 
persist until adulthood in the liver (Fig. 2a)70. HSCs are the pericytes of 
the liver. They localize in the space of Disse (Box 1) between hepatocytes 
and liver endothelial cells, in proximity to Kupffer cells. Quiescent HSCs 
store vitamin A in lipid droplets to support cell growth, intracellular 
metabolism, immune responses and regeneration71. Accordingly, their 
gene signature is characterized by the expression of lecithin retinol 
acyltransferase (LRAT) and phodiesterase 3B (PDE3B) (for vitamin A 
storage and metabolism), hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) (supporting 
liver regeneration and hepatocyte differentiation), VEGF (which induces 
the proliferation of endothelial cells) and BMP activin membrane-bound 
inhibitor (BAMBI) (suppressing TGF signalling)72,73. Another protein 
commonly expressed by HSCs is retinol-binding protein 1 (RBP1). How-
ever, its expression is not exclusive to HSCs, as it is also found in several 
other cell types within the liver71. The other mesenchymal cells near 
the liver portal central vein are the portal fibroblasts; they upregulate 
complement pathways contributing to liver immunoregulatory func-
tion and express genes associated with lipid metabolism (for example, 
AOX1), suggesting a role in liver fat breakdown72. Portal fibroblasts also 
contribute to ECM remodelling by producing elastin and proteins that 
support collagen crosslinking74. In addition, they help to maintain the 
peribiliary stem cell niche, encouraging the growth and repair of bile 
duct epithelial cells74,75. It is not yet clear whether the peri-portal fibro-
blasts near the bile duct represent an additional fibroblast subtype72,74,76. 
Finally, the liver has the remarkable ability to regenerate after injury 
when activated fibroblasts, myofibroblasts and activated HSCs are 
cleared through apoptosis or senescence (Box 2). This regeneration is 
aided by portal fibroblasts, which play an important role in resolving 
fibrosis by restoring the balance between matrix metalloproteinases 
(MMPs) and tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs)77.
Intestinal tract
The intestine is involved in nutrients and water absorption, the expul-
sion of waste and pathogen defence. These tasks are primarily per-
formed by the epithelium whose self-renewal capability is attributed 
to LGR5+ intestinal epithelial stem cells (IESCs)78. Intestinal fibroblasts 
support the epithelium and maintain its integrity and homeostasis. 
Hence, we can distinguish different functional fibroblast subtypes that 
occupy distinct niches of the mucosa (lamina propria) of the intestine 
and the sub-epithelial fibroblasts (Fig. 2a), which are closely associ-
ated with the basement membrane of the epithelium. Studies in mice 
have shown that, depending on whether sub-epithelial fibroblasts 
are located in the crypt or villi (small intestine) or upper crypt (large 
intestine), they are genotypically and functionally distinct and form 
an opposing BMP–WNT signalling gradient to support epithelial dif-
ferentiation at the top of the crypt/villi versus IESC maintenance at 
the crypt bottom (reviewed in ref. 79) (Fig. 2a). Studies on intestinal 
fibroblast subtypes in humans have identified distinct populations 
located near the epithelial layer in the crypt niche of the colon with 
similar signatures to those in mice80–82. Sub-epithelial SOX6+ fibro-
blasts form a unique mesenchymal niche that supports epithelial 
cell regeneration37,81. These cells express collagen genes (COL4A5 and 
COL4A6) and genes transcribing BMP ligands (BMP2 and BMP5), Wnt 
ligands (WNT5 and WNT5A) and the Wnt antagonist (FRZB), reflecting 
active BMP signalling that restricts IESC self-renewal in favour of epi-
thelial differentiation81,82. Other fibroblast populations spanning the 
colon mucosa overlap in their expression of GREM1, GREM2, RSPO3 and 
WNT2B, the signature genes of bottom crypt fibroblasts in mice81–83. 
This includes OGN+ fibroblasts, which contribute to connective tis-
sue generation (DCN) and structural integrity (THBS4 and TNC)82,83. 
Similarly, APOE+ZEB2+ fibroblasts are involved in ECM dynamics and 
maintenance of tissue elasticity and express non-fibrillar collagens 
(COL14A1 and COL15A1) and elastic fibres (FN1)81–83. This population also 
expresses ADAMDEC1 and may overlap with CXCL14+ fibroblasts, which 
have an immune modulatory role, producing inflammatory mediators 
(for example, CXCL12, CXCL14 and CCL8)81,83. Immune regulation is also 
a feature of CCL19+ fibroblasts, which recruit and activate immune cells 
via CCL21, TNFSF14, TNFRSF13B and IL33 (ref. 82). Another subtype, 
marked by canonical WNT ligands (WNT2 and WNT2B) and the WNT 
signalling enhancer RSPO3, promotes IESC proliferation and stem cell 
niche maintenance. This occurs through BMP antagonists (GREM1, 
GREM2 and NOG), counteracting BMP signalling to ensure a steady 
renewal of the intestinal lining79–83. Although some mucosa/lamina pro-
pria fibroblasts share overlapping gene signatures, it remains unclear 
whether they represent distinct subtypes or a single population with 
diverse functions. Intestine homeostasis is also associated with an 
immunomodulatory function that is absolved by the gut microbiome, 
fibroblasts and epithelial cells, which form a sophisticated immune 
regulatory network in mice that may play a role in humans84–86. For 
instance, gut microbiota ferment fibres to produce short-chain fatty 
acids such as butyrate, which influence the function of human immor-
talized fibroblasts in vitro. These fibroblasts, in turn, modulate immune 
responses, including those of regulatory T cells86. Short-chain fatty 
acids also affect myofibroblast activity, promoting prostaglandin and 
mucin (MUC2) production, which protects the epithelial layer from 
inflammation. Decreased expression of MUC2 and MUC3 has been 
linked to the progression of inflammatory bowel diseases (IBDs)87,88. 
These interactions highlight the critical role of fibroblasts in the gut’s 
immune landscape. An intriguing question is whether immune regula-
tory fibroblast subtypes, such as CCL19+ or ADAMDEC1+, interact with 
the microbiome, and if so how such interactions could be harnessed to 
mitigate inflammation and prevent disease progression. Finally, a sub-
set of intestinal myofibroblasts expressing contractile genes (ACTA2+ 
TAGLN+MYH11+) is located near the crypt base and mucosa where these 
cells are actively involved in ECM remodelling triggered by TGFβ81,82.
Fibroblast plasticity under non-homeostatic 
conditions
Fibroblasts exhibit remarkable plasticity, allowing them to adapt their 
activities and modify their gene signatures in response to disturbances 

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
728
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
in homeostasis. However, it remains to be investigated whether all fibro-
blast subtypes have the potential to adopt functional roles or whether 
developmental determinants restrict certain fibroblast populations to 
specific functions. In this chapter, we examine fibrotic and inflamma-
tory fibroblast subtypes that emerge in pathological contexts.
Inflammatory fibroblasts
Inflammatory fibroblasts represent an activated subset of steady-state 
fibroblasts that undergo phenotypic shifts in response to local and 
systemic inflammatory stimuli, acting as key mediators across various 
inflammatory conditions. These cells contribute to diseases, such as 
atopic dermatitis, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, IBD and rheumatoid 
arthritis, through the production of and response to pro-inflammatory 
cytokines and chemokines19,89,90. For instance, the most common inflam-
matory population are CCL19+ fibroblasts, which are frequently found 
in lymphocyte niches of various inflamed tissues, including the synovial 
joints (rheumatoid arthritis), colon (IBD) and lung and skin (systemic 
scleroderma) (Fig. 2b and Box 3)19,48,80. These fibroblasts overlap in 
their expression of interferon-stimulated genes (for example, CXCL9 
and CXCL10) and facilitate lymphocyte chemotaxis and proliferation 
favouring type 2 immunity81,91. Similarly, in rheumatoid arthritis, THY1+ 
(HLA-DR+) fibroblasts exhibit heightened major histocompatibility 
complex class II expression in response to interferon-γ, which influences 
immune responses19. Another THY1+ (PDPN+FAP+) inflammatory fibro-
blast population is also found in advanced ulcerative colitis. These cells 
are located in the neutrophil influx area, are recruited via interleukin-1 
(IL-1) receptor signalling and secrete neutrophil-attracting chemokines 
(CXCL1 and CXCL2)92.
Interestingly, IL-13Rα2+IL-11+ cells—the inflammatory fibroblast 
population identified in ulcerative colitis—exhibit functional plastic-
ity and express many genes associated with colitis, fibrosis and cancer 
(IL24, FAP and WNT2), suggesting a similarity between inflammatory 
fibroblasts and cancer cells80. Transitional states between inflammatory 
and ECM-remodelling states found in inflammatory cancer-associated 
fibroblasts are regulated by TGFβ and IL-6 signalling93. This duality is 
also observed in hyaluronan synthase 1-positive (HAS1+) fibroblasts 
found in pulmonary fibrosis and pre-metastatic niches in mice (Ptg
s2+Has1high)60,94. HAS1+ fibroblasts participate in immune functions 
(IL-4–IL-13 signalling) and matrix functions (collagen degradation, 
elastic fibre formation and ECM organization)60,94. Of note, IL-4–IL-13 
signalling is one of the key pathways in wound healing and tumour pro-
gression, both of which involve inflammation and tissue remodelling. 
Indeed, in lesional atopic dermatitis, IL-4–IL-13 signalling mediates the 
communication between COL6A5+COL18A1+ fibroblasts and T cells. In 
atopic dermatitis, COL6A5+COL18A1+ fibroblasts express inflammatory 
cytokines (CCL19 and CCL21) and ECM products (POSTN and TCN) as a 
response to type 2 inflammation90.
Across multiple tissues, fibroblast clusters exhibit overlapping 
inflammatory (tumour necrosis factor (TNF) and IL-6) and myofibro-
blastic (TGFβ and Wnt) features, highlighting their role in translating 
inflammatory signals into tissue responses37. Consistently, we recently 
observed in wound healing studies in mice and human skin samples that 
during scar formation, fascia fibroblasts transition into myofibroblasts 
through a pro-inflammatory state39. This suggests that inflamma-
tory fibroblasts are central to tissue repair and the remodelling phase 
of inflammation. In summary, inflammatory fibroblasts induced in 
non-homeostatic tissues often share common activation signatures 
and functions across different organs and represent a functional con-
tinuum rather than strictly discrete populations. Shared marker genes 
(for example, CCL19 and THY1) and pathways (for example, nuclear 
factor-κB and interferon signalling) indicate interconnected roles in 
promoting and sustaining inflammation. However, their precise func-
tions are influenced by their microenvironment, whether lymphoid, 
perivascular or epithelial.
BOX 2
Fibroblast with pro-regenerative potential
Tissue regeneration refers to a process by which organisms repair 
or replace damaged tissues, restoring their structure and function. 
In most adult mammals, including mice and humans, the capacity 
for tissue regeneration is limited. In this context, pro-regenerative 
fibroblasts represent a subset of fibroblasts with unique gene 
transcription profiles that distinguish them from pro-fibrotic 
fibroblasts. These cells contribute to tissue regeneration by 
balancing ECM deposition and degradation and restoring normal 
tissue structure and function through the secretion of growth factors 
such as vascular endothelial growth factor A (which supports 
angiogenesis) and TGFβ3 (which modulates inflammation and 
fibrosis following injury)134. Pro-regenerative fibroblasts activate 
essential regenerative pathways, including PDGF and FGF signalling. 
Research into regenerative fibroblasts across internal organs has 
been thoroughly reviewed elsewhere135.
Certain species, such as the spiny mouse (Acomys), are 
characterized by their exceptional and rare regenerative capacity 
among mammals and are an excellent model for studying the 
regenerative capabilities of fibroblasts. For instance, following 
injury, transient αSMA+ myofibroblasts differentiate into various 
mesenchymal cell types and form new hair follicles, a process 
not observed in conventional murine models such as Mus 
musculus. Additionally, the ECM in Acomys primarily comprises 
collagen type III instead of type I, creating a softer environment 
conducive to regeneration and correlating with an increase in 
Lum+ fibroblasts136,137. In humans, pro-regenerative fibroblasts 
are an emerging area of research, with much of the foundational 
knowledge coming from animal models. However, certain 
human fibroblasts display remarkable regenerative capabilities. 
CRABP1+ fibroblasts, present predominantly in foetal and to a 
lesser extent adult human skin, possess a unique regenerative 
profile. These cells are linked to decreased inflammation and 
are responsive to Hedgehog signalling, driving differentiation 
into dermal papilla cells138,139. These findings suggest that unique 
regenerative properties are associated with CRABP1 expression. 
Buccal fibroblasts, found in the oral cavity, exemplify regenerative 
properties in human tissue, exhibit high proliferative capacity and 
contribute to vascular remodelling, blood vessel formation and 
ECM assembly43. Their regenerative ability is regulated by the Wnt 
signalling pathway, which promotes rapid tissue repair in the oral 
cavity that is consistently exposed to mechanical stress43,140,141.
Additionally, fibroblasts play a pivotal role in regeneration across 
the intestine and liver, which exhibit remarkable regenerative 
capacities. In the intestine, WNT2B+RSPO3+ fibroblasts’ regenerative 
capacity is associated with their role in LGR5+ intestinal stem cell niche 
support and maintenance. In the liver, HSCs and fibroblasts contribute 
to regeneration by reverting fibrosis. This occurs through apoptosis 
or senescence, restoring the balance between TIMPs and MMPs. 
ECM remodelling also plays a crucial role; for example, vitronectin 
and olfactomedin 4 (OLFM4) in injured livers enhance cell migration 
and adhesion, whereas FN1 and collagen I promote proliferation and 
differentiation of liver progenitor cells in mice and humans142.

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
729
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
Myofibroblasts and fibrotic fibroblasts
Fibrotic fibroblasts and myofibroblasts present activation states, which 
can be found in physiological conditions (normal wound healing) and 
diseases (chronic wound healing, tissue fibrosis and cancer). In response 
to an injury, fibroblasts become activated, gain migratory capacity, 
produce ECM proteins and become proto-myofibroblasts (for example, 
at the early stages of wound healing)95. These proto-myofibroblasts 
represent an intermediate stage before maturing into contractile myofi-
broblasts, which remodel the ECM to support immune cell infiltration 
and further ECM production95,96. Fully mature myofibroblasts highly 
express α-smooth muscle actin (αSMA), which integrates in stress fibres. 
They develop a contractile phenotype that plays a key role in wound 
contraction and the reorganization of collagen fibres within the scar95.
During the normal wound healing process, myofibroblasts exist 
in a transient state and are called transient myofibroblasts. However, 
under continued stimulation by TGFβ, transient myofibroblasts sta-
bilize into persistent forms with increased fibrogenic activity, which 
deposit less matrix and are mainly involved in wound contraction. 
Persistent myofibroblasts are responsible for pathological scarring 
and fibrosis and support immunosuppression niche formation in 
cancer37,95,97. The majority of myofibroblasts develop from acti-
vated fibroblasts. However, other cell types, such as epithelial cells 
(via epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition), pericytes and specific 
mesenchymal progenitors, can also generate myofibroblasts95,98–100 
(Fig. 2b). Whether or not adipocytes can transdifferentiate (Box 1) and 
develop into myofibroblasts is still a topic of debate101,102. ACTA2 is often 
reported as the marker gene for myofibroblasts, although it does not 
define myofibroblasts in all fibrotic niches and can be expressed by 
other stromal cells. Recent transcriptomic studies have indicated alveo-
lar CTHRC1+ cells as a pathologic fibroblast population that is almost 
absent in healthy lungs, and that they represent the major source of 
myofibroblasts in lung fibrosis, particularly in cases related to COVID-
19 (refs. 51,54,103). Although CTHRC1+ myofibroblasts express ACTA2, 
this marker is also expressed in non-fibroblastic niches in the lung51,54.
BOX 3
Fibroblast diversity underlies human diseases
Hypertrophic scars and keloids. Hypertrophic scars and keloids 
are both types of abnormal scar tissue. Hypertrophic scars are due to 
excessive collagen deposition after wound closure, whereas keloids 
are aberrant scars exceeding the wound size due to excessive prolif-
eration of granulation tissue and collagen type I and/or III bundles143. 
When comparing typical hypertrophic scars with keloids, it appears 
that in hypertrophic scars the CD39+ papillary fibroblasts expand, 
and their increase is correlated with disease severity. Additionally, 
CD39+ fibroblasts secrete IL-11 through TGFβ signalling, which induces 
a dermal fibroblast fibrotic phenotype and αSMA+CD39− myofibro-
blast differentiation91,122. By contrast, CD266+CD9− myofibroblasts 
predominate in keloids and these cells express genes involved in 
matrix deposition (POSTN, ASPN and COL11A1)122. At the same time, 
fibroblasts in keloids exhibit elevated levels of DPP4, FAP and NOV—
markers identified in murine fascia fibroblasts99. This suggests that 
during fibrosis persistent myofibroblasts may originate from the 
fascia layer99.
Scleroderma. Systemic scleroderma is a complex autoimmune dis-
ease marked by widespread organ fibrosis. Central to this process 
are persistent myofibroblasts—particularly COL8A1+ and COL11A1+ 
myofibroblasts—producing ECM but also expressing high levels of 
collagen genes and engaging in pro-fibrotic signalling124,125. In the 
skin, these myofibroblasts originate from SFRP2+ fibroblasts in a 
process driven by dysregulation of the Hippo pathway, leading to the 
activation of YAP/TAZ, which promotes pro-fibrotic gene expression 
and increases ECM stiffness, establishing a vicious fibrotic cycle. 
Additionally, the TGFβ–SMAD3 and Wnt signalling pathways amplify 
this fibrotic response106,124 (Fig. 2a). Persistent myofibroblasts also 
arise from endothelial cells through endothelial-to-mesenchymal 
transition. The contribution of these cells to fibrosis is associated 
with their interactions with COL8A1+ and CCL19+ pro-inflammatory 
fibroblasts48,124,125. Moreover, the exacerbation of fibrosis in systemic 
scleroderma correlates with the loss of CXCL12+ and PI16+ steady-state 
fibroblasts, which is essential for maintaining tissue balance and vas-
cularization124. Interestingly, recent studies have downplayed the role 
of LRG5+ fibroblasts previously associated with disease progression, 
suggesting that its complexity remains incompletely understood123,125.
Inflammatory bowel disease. IBD is an umbrella term for chronic 
disorders that cause inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, 
such as Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis. Both diseases exhibit 
epithelial barrier defects and mucosal immune dysregulation82. In 
Crohn’s disease, excessive fibrosis is associated with an increase in 
TIMPs and a decrease in MMPs126,127. Disease progression is mediated 
by hyperactivation of fibrogenic αSMA+TAGLN+ myofibroblasts in 
response to chronic inflammation associated with an expansion of 
CCL19+CCL21+ inflammatory fibroblasts and WNT2B+ and TNFRSF13B+ 
fibroblasts80–82. In IBD, immune cells and inflammatory fibroblasts 
create a sustained inflammatory loop via activation of the JAK–STAT 
signalling pathway. In mice, activation in intestinal fibroblasts of the 
YAP/TAZ pathway has been associated with the production of fibro-
genic markers (Col1a1 and Ctgf), leading to intestinal obstruction127.
Ulcerative colitis primarily affects the colon and rectum and is 
associated with an expansion of IL-13Rα2+ and IL-11+ inflammatory 
fibroblasts80. In this disease, TNFRSF13B+ fibroblasts highly express 
genes inducing lymphocyte migration and recruitment of immune 
cells (IL11 and IL24), contributing to epithelial barrier breakdown80,81. 
Of interest, the reduction of WNT5A+ epithelial proximal fibroblasts, 
which play a crucial role in maintaining the epithelial niche, in patients’ 
biopsies is associated with the compromised epithelial barrier 
observed in ulcerative colitis81,82.
Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is a persistent synovial 
membrane inflammation that leads to progressive joint damage144. 
Two main subsets of synovial fibroblasts have been identified in rheu-
matoid arthritis—sublining and lining fibroblasts129,130—and several 
subpopulations of sublining fibroblasts have been characterized 
(Table 2). Sublining fibroblasts, especially in leukocyte-rich rheu-
matoid arthritis, are known for their inflammatory responses (IL6, 
CCL2 and PTGS2); they recruit and activate macrophages and T cells, 
perpetuating inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis joints19,129–131. 
A recent study revealed that THY1+CD34+ synovial fibroblasts undergo 
metabolic reprogramming after complement C3a–C3aR1 interac-
tion131. Enhanced glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation in synovial 
fibroblasts are associated with inflammasome activation, IL-1β release 
and hypoxia-inducible factor 1-α (HIF1α)-mediated pro-survival sig-
nals that protect against senescence. Conversely, lining fibroblasts are 
associated with tissue remodelling and ECM degradation mediated by 
Wnt signalling, which supports cartilage destruction in rheumatoid 
arthritis pathology130,132; they are characterized by a lack of THY1 and 
expression of FAP130.

Nature Cell Biology | Volume 27 | May 2025 | 720–735
731
Review article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-025-01638-5
Fibroblast activation and the formation of stress fibres drive their 
transformation into myofibroblasts and involve multiple signalling 
pathways that are similar across organs, regardless of the disease or 
myofibroblast precursors. A key driver of this process is TGFβ, which 
activates various pathways, including SMAD, MAPK–ERK and PI3K–
AKT–mTOR104. These pathways promote the transcription of ECM com-
ponents and contraction-related genes. Additionally, TGFβ suppresses 
the activity of proteases responsible for ECM degradation, thereby 
amplifying the fibrotic response95,105. Furthermore, dysregulation of 
the Wnt signalling pathway plays a critical role in fibroblast activa-
tion (for example, in SFRP2+ fibroblasts)106. Loss of the Wnt inhibitor 
WIF1 leads to uncontrolled Wnt signalling, driving myofibroblast dif-
ferentiation and ECM accumulation46,106. Additionally, the ECM itself 
generates mechanical cues that perpetuate myofibroblast activation. 
In response to biomechanical tension in injured tissue, fibroblasts 
activate mechanosensitive proteins, such as Yes-associated protein 
(YAP) and transcriptional co-activator with PDZ-binding motif (TAZ), 
which subsequently translocate to the nucleus where they activate 
genes involved in ECM synthesis and myofibroblast differentiation 
(Fig. 2b). This mechanotransduction reinforces fibrosis by creating a 
stiffer ECM, which further activates fibroblasts and sustains myofibro-
blast activity23,107,108. Moreover, cell–cell interactions also play a crucial 
role in the activity of myofibroblasts. This includes the interaction with 
other fibroblasts, but also with immune cells such as macrophages. 
In murine skin, En1+ fascia fibroblasts exhibit high contractility and 
upregulate the adhesion molecules N-cadherin and connexin43 (Cx43), 
which mediate collective migration of the fibroblasts leading to wound 
closure through skin contraction and scarring109,110. This mechanism 
was also observed when investigating human skin fascia in an ex situ 
skin scar model (scar-like tissue in a dish)39. Of note, gingival fibroblasts 
rapidly downregulate Cx43 during oral wound healing, which correlates 
with minimal scarring, highlighting the tissue-specific behaviour of 
fibroblasts111. Fibroblast activation is further influenced by physical 
interactions with macrophages, which can even override the quiescent 
state of fibroblasts. A recent study revealed that integrin-mediated 
binding between macrophages and fibroblasts triggers a calcium (Ca2+) 
influx in fibroblasts, driving contractile events that lead to collagen 
contraction112. Additionally, previous research has demonstrated that 
direct contact between macrophages and myofibroblasts in fibrotic 
tissues of both humans and mice is mediated by cadherins, such as 
cadherin 11 (CDH11)113. A strong adhesion between the two cell types 
helps to sustain the persistent activation of myofibroblasts under TGFβ 
signalling, as observed in vitro.
Compared with myofibroblasts, fibrotic fibroblasts (Box 1) are 
persistently activated cells primarily involved in ECM deposition (col-
lagen 1) and tissue remodelling and may not exhibit strong contractile 
properties13,23. Fibrotic fibroblasts contribute to chronic scarring in 
fibrotic conditions such as pulmonary fibrosis, liver cirrhosis and sys-
temic sclerosis (Box 3 and Table 2)65,98,114. POSTN has been reported 
as a marker for fibrotic fibroblasts during cardiac fibrosis115. POSTN+ 
fibroblasts express the PCOLCE2 gene linked to ECM reorganization and 
the typical fibrotic makers CTGF and FAP, along with ECM-remodelling 
factors (EDNRA and FRZB) and the transcription factors RUNX1 and 
RUNX2 involved in myofibroblast differentiation115,116. POSTN+ fibrotic 
fibroblasts are abundant in cardiomyopathies, in part due to expression 
of the recently identified transcription factor AEBP1, which promotes 
their expansion68,117,118. In the lung, fibrotic fibroblast populations and 
myofibroblast numbers are increased within multiple lung niches (for 
example, during pulmonary fibrosis). For instance, PLIN2+ and LUM+ 
fibroblasts give origin to myofibroblasts, and together with the recently 
discovered HAS1+ fibrotic fibroblasts these populations expand in idi-
opathic pulmonary fibrosis, whereas their presence is minimal or absent 
in healthy lungs60,61. HAS1+ fibroblasts located in the lung subpleural 
space exhibit an invasive phenotype and may correspond to Ptgs2+ 
adventitial fibroblasts observed in the pre-metastatic niche in mice60,94. 
An interesting feature of these cells is that, as well as certain fibroblasts 
identified in the liver, they show pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic 
abilities, with upregulated inflammatory markers (IL32, CDF1 and CCL2), 
as well as pro-fibrotic markers (ACTA2, TAGLN and SPARC)60,72,119. In sum-
mary, the coordinated response of fibrotic fibroblasts and myofibro-
blasts underscores a core theme across organs where these cells act as 
mediators of tissue repair and pathological remodelling.

Conclusions and future perspectives
Understanding fibroblast diversity and functionality in human tissue is 
crucial for advancing our knowledge of tissue homeostasis and repair 
mechanisms in disease. In this Review, we underscore the existence of 
heterogeneous fibroblast populations within and between organs that 
are shaped by both environmental cues and their embryonic origins, 
highlighting the complexity of these cells in maintaining tissue home-
ostasis. We also emphasize that diseases are not solely driven by the 
newly activated inflammatory or fibrotic fibroblast populations. Instead, 
they mainly arise from the loss or dysfunction of steady-state fibroblast 
populations that are essential for maintaining healthy tissue architecture 
and function. Future research will help to uncover previously unknown 
information regarding fibroblast function in tissue homeostasis and 
diseases, elucidating the complex interplay between various fibroblast 
subtypes. These advances will allow the development of targeted thera-
peutic strategies to restore normal fibroblast function and ultimately 
improve patient outcomes in inflammatory and fibrotic diseases.

===== Tran2018_PhysiolRev_SCIregeneration+Biology =====
THE BIOLOGY OF REGENERATION FAILURE AND
SUCCESS AFTER SPINAL CORD INJURY
Amanda Phuong Tran, Philippa Mary Warren, and Jerry Silver
Department of Neurosciences, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio; and School of Biomedical
Sciences, University of Leeds, Leeds, United Kingdom
L
Tran AP, Warren PM, Silver J. The Biology of Regeneration Failure and Success After
Spinal Cord Injury. Physiol Rev 98: 881–917, 2018. Published March 7, 2018;
doi:10.1152/physrev.00017.2017.—Since no approved therapies to restore mobil-
ity and sensation following spinal cord injury (SCI) currently exist, a better understanding
of the cellular and molecular mechanisms following SCI that compromise regeneration
or neuroplasticity is needed to develop new strategies to promote axonal regrowth and restore
function. Physical trauma to the spinal cord results in vascular disruption that, in turn, causes
blood-spinal cord barrier rupture leading to hemorrhage and ischemia, followed by rampant local
cell death. As subsequent edema and inﬂammation occur, neuronal and glial necrosis and apopto-
sis spread well beyond the initial site of impact, ultimately resolving into a cavity surrounded by
glial/ﬁbrotic scarring. The glial scar, which stabilizes the spread of secondary injury, also acts as
a chronic, physical, and chemo-entrapping barrier that prevents axonal regeneration. Understand-
ing the formative events in glial scarring helps guide strategies towards the development of
potential therapies to enhance axon regeneration and functional recovery at both acute and chronic
stages following SCI. This review will also discuss the perineuronal net and how chondroitin sulfate
proteoglycans (CSPGs) deposited in both the glial scar and net impede axonal outgrowth at the level
of the growth cone. We will end the review with a summary of current CSPG-targeting strategies
that help to foster axonal regeneration, neuroplasticity/sprouting, and functional recovery following
SCI.

CONCLUDING REMARKS
904
I. INTRODUCTION
When asked in an anonymous questionnaire whether emer-
gency care professionals would desire resuscitative mea-
sures following a severe spinal cord injury (SCI), only 37%
of physicians and 14% of nurses indicated that they them-
selves would want measures taken to ensure survival (138).
This bleak perception among health care providers of life
following SCI derives not only from the devastating perma-
nence of such an injury, but also underscores the dearth of
available treatments. In the time that has elapsed since this
survey was conducted in the 1990s, our understanding of
the molecular mechanisms underlying regenerative failure
after SCI has greatly improved, providing hope for novel
treatments. Indeed, SCI research has come a long way from
Ramon y Cajal’s descriptions of largely abortive regenera-
tion of the “sterile clubs” of severed axonal tips transiently
stalled in the glial scar which he believed were destined to
die away (56). David and Aguayo’s (81) peripheral nerve
graft experiments provided an important appreciation that
certain types of central nervous system (CNS) axons do
possess some level of an intrinsic ability to regenerate, at
least acutely after injury, when presented with a hospitable
environment. This galvanized new hope that additional SCI
research could lead to functional improvements.
There are ~17,000 new cases of SCI recorded each year in
the United States with motor vehicle collisions still contrib-
uting to the most common etiology of traumatic cord injury
(279a). Alarmingly, since 2012 as the population ages, the
number of recorded cases of traumatic SCI due to falls has
risen from 16 to 30.5% (92). Clinical presentation is depen-
dent on the severity and location of injury since the long
tracts within the cord are arranged somatotopically as they
interconnect the brain with autonomic and peripheral ner-
vous systems. Most current acute treatments aim to stabi-
lize the cord and restore homeostasis immediately following
injury while long-term treatments mainly seek to manage
symptoms arising from maladaptive plasticity and other
secondary complications (65, 101, 199).
Our understanding of the extrinsic and intrinsic factors that
block axonal regeneration or neuroplasticity has vastly im-
proved with greater elucidation of the course of molecular
events that occur following SCI in animal models. How-
Physiol Rev 98: 881–917, 2018
Published March 7, 2018; doi:10.1152/physrev.00017.2017
881
0031-9333/18 Copyright © 2018 the American Physiological Society
Downloaded from journals.physiology.org/journal/physrev (175.122.076.013) on July 1, 2021.

ever, we need to keep in mind that variation of inﬂamma-
tory and subsequent glial responses exists between mice and
rats (365), different genetic mouse backgrounds (202, 243),
and rat strains (312). There is variability in the mechanics of
the different injury models as well as deviations in severities
of the same injury type (369). In this review, we describe
how primary and subsequent secondary injuries contribute
to the formation of the glial scar (FIGURE 1). Major cellular
components of the glial scar including microglia and pe-
ripherally derived leukocytes, astrocytes, oligodendrocyte
progenitors, ependymal cells, and pericytes/ﬁbroblasts will
be further described in detail. We then discuss how the
matrix contents of the glial scar, notably chondroitin sulfate
proteoglycans (CSPGs), inhibit axon outgrowth and func-
tional recovery. We also review the inhibitory role of CSPGs
in the perineuronal net (PNN). Given that the molecular
and cellular cascade of events and possible repair strategies
are so expansive, we focus our review from our unique
perspective based on modulation of the glial scar by miti-
gating the inhibitory potential of CSPGs, as well as combi-
natorial strategies that target the glial scar.
II. DESCRIPTION OF SCI-INDUCED
PRIMARY AND SECONDARY INJURY
In rodent models of contusive SCI, the vertebrae and sur-
rounding laminae are excised to expose the cord. The de-
sired amount of force is translated through a clip, piston, or
a set amount of weight that is impelled or dropped onto the
protracted cord, which causes immediate damage that ex-
pands in an elliptical shape rostrally and caudally from the
center of impact. While there is some variability in the ex-
tent of spared tissue, this model has been useful in recapit-
ulating major morphological and cellular changes present
in traumatic human SCI (369). In the contusive model of
injury, the force of impact rapidly displaces the cord (FIG-
URE 2, A–C). While physical trauma is usually brief, tissue
remodeling starts immediately, peaks weeks after injury,
and persists for the remainder of life.
A. Physical Trauma and Primary Injury
Physical impact to the spinal cord causes symmetrically
expanding foci of glial and neuronal necrosis within the
lesion epicenter (FIGURE 2F) (146). Necrosis is a disor-
dered phenomenon of cell death characterized by somal
swelling, loss of cytoplasmic deﬁnition, chromatin aggre-
gation that may leak into the cytoplasm, and spewed organ-
elles resulting in ejection of proteins that contribute to the
inﬂammatory milieu (18). These include the release of
alarmins that initiate a reactive state in resident glia and the
downstream inﬁltration of immune cells from the periphery
(FIGURE 3) (33).
In contrast, apoptosis is an ordered, ATP-driven process of
cell death marked by tightly packed condensation of the cell
body that elicits a far less potent inﬂammatory signal than
necrosis (FIGURE 2E) (209). Neuronal apoptosis seems con-
ﬁned to the early stages of injury (147). However, beyond
24 h post trauma, glial apoptosis persists while neuronal
apoptosis diminishes (74, 237). Among glia, astrocytes
seem most refractory to apoptosis (146) while oligodendro-
cyte apoptosis persists in distant white matter several days
or longer after impact (29, 237, 374).
Physical impact additionally causes descending and ascend-
ing axons along the lesion to shear and then degenerate
(277). These severed axons, separated from their somata,
undergo stereotypical Wallerian degeneration of the distal
aspect of the axon as well as their associated myelin sheaths.
Successive axonal dieback (49, 171) due to aggressive mac-
rophage activity (70, 201) and demyelination (41) also oc-
cur in the proximal segment which, in turn, may contribute
to the list of factors that limit collateral sprouting (75).

FIGURE 1.
Overview of spinal cord injury
pathophysiology. Spinal cord injury can be
divided among four progressive stages:
physical trauma, primary injury, secondary
injury, which ultimately creates a chroni-
cally axon-inhibitory structure called the
glial scar.

Understanding the homeostatic and protective functions of
the blood-spinal cord barrier (BSCB) in keeping peripheral
immune cells, toxic metabolic products, and other inﬂam-
matory substances excluded from the CNS emphasizes the
extent of disruption that SCI incurs. The BSCB comprises
nonfenestrated endothelial cells which form tight junctions
with each other to wrap around blood vessels. The next
layer includes endothelial cell-supporting pericytes embed-
ded in the basal lamina which are all enveloped by the
endfeet of astrocyte processes (182) that play a major role in
tightening the endothelial barrier (24). Upon injury, endo-
thelial cells shed their glycocalyx, and tight junctions are

and water. Of course, a major complication of BSCB and
blood vessel rupture is hemorrhaging (FIGURE 2D), notably
in the grey matter (41, 211). Bleeding occurs within minutes
after SCI (18), but can last for days (265) contributing to the
cavitation of the lesion site which can further potentiate

FIGURE 3.
Secondary injury. Secondary injury is marked by inﬂammation initiated by physical injury and
release of alarmins. A–C: leukocyte inﬁltration through a compromised BSCB and subsequent differentiation can
be seen in the lesion of the mouse spinal cord after injury. While both M1 (CD16/32) and M2 (arginase 1)
macrophages are present, the M1 type dominates and persists. [From Kigerl et al. (201), with permission from
Society for Neuroscience conveyed through Copyright Clearance Center, Inc.] D and E: M1-like macrophages may
induce further damage to axons through dieback, or contact-based retraction of the axon to the soma as seen with
peripheral rat axons in vitro. However, immune modulatory treatments such as MAPC stem cells may push
